source_name: Mistakes Were Made (Substack) - Ari Shtein, Yale and Columbia admitted student
text:
Source:
Mistakes Were Made (Substack) - Ari Shtein, Yale and Columbia admitted student
https://mistakesweremade.substack.com/p/my-college-application-essay

School:
Yale / Columbia

Advice:
Ari Shtein, who was admitted to both Yale and Columbia, offered pointed commentary on what sank a widely-discussed application in 2024. The applicant in question had impressive credentials including a business generating tens of millions in revenue, a strong GPA, and solid test scores, but was rejected everywhere. Shtein and others who analyzed the situation pointed to the essay as the primary problem. The essay came across as self-congratulatory and framed the applicant as someone with an inflated sense of his own importance. Shtein noted that the first rule of college application essays is not to come across as arrogant. He also listed other common essay pitfalls: starting nearly every sentence with I, inserting SAT vocabulary words to appear smart, ending with a tidy life lesson that sounds formulaic, and writing about a deceased grandparent as the central subject.

Key Takeaway:
Even extraordinary credentials cannot overcome an essay that makes an admissions reader dislike you. Sounding arrogant, formulaic, or inauthentic in your essay is one of the most reliable ways to hurt an otherwise strong application. The essay is your personality on paper, and personality matters enormously.

source_name: Harvard College Student Stories Blog (college.harvard.edu) - Braeden, Class of 2021
text:
Source:
Harvard College Student Stories Blog (college.harvard.edu) - Braeden, Class of 2021
https://college.harvard.edu/student-life/student-stories/my-admissions-journey

School:
Harvard

Advice:
Braeden reflected on his admissions process and identified applying to twelve schools as a mistake. He paid application fees out of pocket and watched his savings dwindle applying to schools ranked highly on websites but schools he was not genuinely passionate about. He also said that applying to schools for other people's expectations rather than your own genuine interest was a fundamental error. He felt caught between the schools others expected him to want and the schools that actually suited him.

Key Takeaway:
Applying to schools because they rank well or because others expect you to, rather than because you genuinely want to attend, is a waste of time, money, and emotional energy. Build a school list based on real fit. Having too many schools on your list can also dilute the quality of your supplemental essays.

text:
Source:
Harvard College Student Stories Blog (college.harvard.edu) - Harvard student, "My Top Tips for Applying to Harvard"
https://college.harvard.edu/student-life/student-stories/my-top-tips-applying-harvard

School:
Harvard

Advice:
This Harvard student described going through many essay prompts before finding the right approach. The mistake was trying to write what the admissions office wanted to hear rather than what was authentically true for the applicant. Every time the student tried to write toward an imagined reader's preferences, the essay felt hollow. Once the student started writing about experiences that were genuinely most important to them personally, the essay improved dramatically and revealed a clear sense of who they were.

Key Takeaway:
One of the most common essay mistakes is writing for an imaginary ideal reader rather than writing honestly about yourself. Admissions officers read tens of thousands of essays and can tell immediately when a student is performing rather than sharing. Authenticity is both harder and more effective than calculated impression management.

source_name: IvyWise KnowledgeBase (ivywise.com) - Former MIT admissions officer perspective
text:
Source:
IvyWise KnowledgeBase (ivywise.com) - Former MIT admissions officer perspective
https://www.ivywise.com/ivywise-knowledgebase/10-college-application-mistakes-to-avoid/

School:
Multiple Ivy League / MIT

Advice:
A former MIT admissions officer writing for IvyWise described several mistakes that sent applications to the rejection pile quickly. One major error was students leaving out important personal context. Applicants from demanding home environments, who worked part-time jobs to support their families or cared for younger siblings, sometimes failed to mention this context at all, leaving admissions readers to compare them unfairly against students with far more resources and free time. Another common mistake was writing one-sided essays that focused entirely on what the college could do for the applicant without addressing what the applicant would contribute. A third mistake was lacking vision: students who said they wanted to attend a prestigious school in order to get a good job after graduation made little impression compared to students who articulated a compelling direction for their future.

Key Takeaway:
Failing to provide context about your life circumstances is a significant missed opportunity. Admissions committees cannot give you credit for overcoming obstacles they do not know about. Additionally, essays that are all about receiving rather than giving signal a transactional mindset that does not impress selective schools.

document_description: This document compiles advice, experiences, and reflections from students admitted to Ivy League schools, drawn from public discussions, student blogs, YouTube interviews, and admissions-focused online communities. source_name: IvyWise KnowledgeBase (ivywise.com) - Admissions counselor, formerly of University of Pennsylvania
text:
Source:
IvyWise KnowledgeBase (ivywise.com) - Admissions counselor, formerly of University of Pennsylvania
https://www.ivywise.com/ivywise-knowledgebase/10-college-application-mistakes-to-avoid/

School:
UPenn / Multiple

Advice:
Former admissions counselors noted that one of the most damaging and embarrassing mistakes an applicant can make is submitting a Why This School essay that mentions the wrong school's name. This happens when students copy and paste from one supplement to another and forget to change the school name. Being rejected from MIT because your essay mentions Harvard is entirely avoidable and signals carelessness. Similarly, submitting a generic Why This School essay that could apply to any institution shows the admissions office that you did not take the time to research the school seriously. The essay should contain specific details about programs, professors, courses, clubs, and campus communities that attract you.

Key Takeaway:
Copying supplemental essays without updating school-specific details is one of the most preventable admissions mistakes. A generic Why This School essay communicates a lack of genuine interest in the institution. Specificity demonstrates that you have done your homework and that your interest is real.

source_name: AdmitSee Blog (admitsee.com) - Princeton applicant reflection
text:
Source:
AdmitSee Blog (admitsee.com) - Princeton applicant reflection
https://www.admitsee.com/blog/college-application-regrets-what-id-do-differently

School:
Princeton

Advice:
A student who had applied to Princeton shared that they were so fixated on one particular feature of the school, a tuition-free gap year program, that they did not take the time to explore other schools with open minds. By the time they realized they might actually love Yale-NUS in Singapore, it was too late to make a compelling case to their family about attending. This tunnel vision on a single aspect of a single school narrowed their options unnecessarily and left them with regret about how they had approached building their college list.

Key Takeaway:
Fixating too heavily on one school or one feature of a school limits your ability to make sound decisions. Build your list with genuine curiosity about multiple institutions. Students who go into the process obsessively focused on a single target school often miss excellent alternatives that might actually be better fits.

document_description: This document compiles advice, experiences, and reflections from students admitted to Ivy League schools, drawn from public discussions, student blogs, YouTube interviews, and admissions-focused online communities. Each entry is sourced from a publicly available post, video, or blog. All advice is paraphrased or summarized from the original sources.
source_name: AdmitSee Blog (admitsee.com) - General advice from admitted students
text:
Source:
AdmitSee Blog (admitsee.com) - General advice from admitted students
https://www.admitsee.com/blog/college-application-regrets-what-id-do-differently

School:
Multiple

Advice:
A commonly shared piece of advice from students who ultimately got into their target schools was that they waited too long to start their essays. Nearly every student interviewed said they wished they had started earlier. Beginning the brainstorming process in the spring of junior year, rather than scrambling in the fall of senior year, allows enough time to write multiple drafts, get feedback, and let ideas evolve naturally. Students who rushed their essays in the final weeks before deadlines consistently felt their applications were weaker because of it.

Key Takeaway:
Starting essays late is one of the most consistent and avoidable mistakes in the Ivy League application process. Rushed essays show. The strongest applications reflect weeks or months of iterative writing and revision, not a last-minute sprint.

source_name: The College Panda (thecollegepanda.com) - Summary of advice from multiple admitted students
text:
Source:
The College Panda (thecollegepanda.com) - Summary of advice from multiple admitted students
https://thecollegepanda.com/admissions-advice-from-students-who-have-overcome-the-odds/

School:
Multiple Ivy League

Advice:
One student quoted in the College Panda compilation said she made the mistake of not sharing her essays with anyone because they were too personal and she felt protective of them. She later called this a significant error. Getting feedback does not mean accepting every suggestion, but it is the only way to know how an essay lands with a real reader. An essay that feels deeply personal and powerful to its author may read as self-indulgent or confusing to someone who does not know you.

Key Takeaway:
Refusing to share your essays out of protectiveness or embarrassment is a mistake. Outside readers, especially those who know you well, can tell you whether the essay actually sounds like you and whether it communicates what you intend. This feedback is essential and cannot be replicated by re-reading your own work.

source_name: Patch.com / College Essay Adviser Observations - Feedback from Ivy League admissions officers relayed by Dr. Paul Lowe
text:
Source:
Patch.com / College Essay Adviser Observations - Feedback from Ivy League admissions officers relayed by Dr. Paul Lowe
https://patch.com/new-york/rye/college-application-essays--avoid-the-stress_39867aa7

School:
Multiple Ivy League

Advice:
Admissions officers at Ivy League schools reported that some of the most common essay failures included essays that sounded as though they were written by the applicant's parents, essays that were verbose and said little of substance, essays that were formulaic and followed a predictable structure, and essays that seemed copied from online samples or published books of successful essays. One admissions officer stated that if an essay was notably weak, both first and second readers would likely flag it and factor it negatively into their recommendation. A badly written essay can definitely hurt an otherwise strong application.

Key Takeaway:
Essays that read as parent-written, formulaic, or modeled too closely on published examples are immediately recognizable to admissions readers. Using AI or over-relying on a counselor's rewrites has similar consequences. Your own authentic voice, even if less polished, is far more effective than a technically perfect essay that sounds like someone else.

document_description: This document compiles advice, experiences, and reflections from students admitted to Ivy League schools, drawn from public discussions, student blogs, YouTube interviews, and admissions-focused online communities. source_name: Patch.com / College Essay Advice - Multiple workshop accounts from admissions advisers
text:
Source:
Patch.com / College Essay Advice - Multiple workshop accounts from admissions advisers
https://patch.com/new-york/rye/college-application-essays--avoid-the-stress_39867aa7

School:
Multiple Ivy League

Advice:
A repeated theme from admissions advisers who had worked with Ivy League applicants was the mistake of trying too hard to sound intellectual. Using a thesaurus to insert elevated vocabulary into an essay does not make a student seem smarter; it makes the writing feel stilted and inauthentic. Admissions officers who read thousands of essays develop a strong sense of when a student is writing naturally versus when they are performing intelligence. Words like plethora, myriad, and copious were flagged specifically as signals of this kind of overreach. Similarly, trying too hard to appear creative by writing highly stylized or abstract essays, comparing one's life to abstract concepts or unusual metaphors, tends to produce essays void of actual personality.

Key Takeaway:
Performing intelligence through vocabulary is a well-known and ineffective strategy. Admissions readers want to understand who you are, not be impressed by your knowledge of synonyms. Write clearly and specifically rather than cleverly and abstractly.

document_description: This document compiles advice, experiences, and reflections from students admitted to Ivy League schools, drawn from public discussions, student blogs, YouTube interviews, and admissions-focused online communities. source_name: Patch.com / College Essay Advice - Multiple workshop accounts
text:
Source:
Patch.com / College Essay Advice - Multiple workshop accounts
https://patch.com/michigan/macomb/bp--applying-to-an-ivy-league-university

School:
Multiple

Advice:
Another frequently cited mistake was writing about a deceased grandparent or another family member as the central subject of the personal statement. The essay prompt is asking about you, not about someone else. Admissions readers see thousands of these essays every cycle. A grandparent's remarkable life does not reveal what makes the applicant distinctive. Unless the subject of a family member is used as a specific lens to examine something deeply personal about the student's own character, values, or development, this topic almost always backfires.

Key Takeaway:
Essays centered on a deceased relative or another person's qualities are extremely common and rarely effective. The application is about you, and any person you write about should serve as a vehicle for illuminating something specific and revealing about yourself. If removing the person from the essay would leave nothing distinctive, the essay needs a different approach.

source_name: Business Insider / College Admissions Expert Commentary - Steve Gardner, Harvard Summer School instructor
text:
Source:
Business Insider / College Admissions Expert Commentary - Steve Gardner, Harvard Summer School instructor
https://new-test-origin.patch.com/connecticut/bethwood/college-application-essay-help-nodx

School:
Yale / Princeton / Harvard

Advice:
Steve Gardner, who has instructed at Harvard and has tracked admissions outcomes closely, challenged a widely held assumption that class rank and GPA are the primary deciding factors at Ivy League schools. One student he guided was admitted to Stanford despite being ranked in the 91st percentile at her school, not at the top of her class. Several classmates ranked above her were rejected. Another student whose essay was about busking in Europe was admitted to Yale, Stanford, and Princeton. Gardner's observation was that students who spend all their energy optimizing their academic record while neglecting the narrative and human elements of their application are making a strategic mistake.

Key Takeaway:
Assuming that a near-perfect GPA or a top class rank is sufficient for Ivy League admission is one of the most common and costly misconceptions. Admissions officers at highly selective schools are building a class, not ranking applicants by GPA. An authentic, revealing application can outperform a technically superior but personality-free one.

source_name: Applying to College (Patch.com) - High school senior blog post, applicant to Cornell, Yale, Brown, Princeton
text:
Source:
Applying to College (Patch.com) - High school senior blog post, applicant to Cornell, Yale, Brown, Princeton
https://patch.com/michigan/macomb/bp--applying-to-an-ivy-league-university

School:
Cornell / Yale / Brown / Princeton

Advice:
A high school senior who was actively applying to several Ivy League schools noted that one underappreciated mistake is failing to research each school's specific test score requirements and submission processes. Some students assume SAT or ACT scores are automatically sent when in fact they must be ordered separately through College Board or ACT directly. Missing this step can delay or compromise an application. The student also noted that many applicants underestimate the weight of the supplemental essays, treating them as secondary to the Common App essay, when in fact they may be the primary mechanism by which admissions offices evaluate fit with their specific institution.

Key Takeaway:
Logistical mistakes like failing to submit test scores through the proper channels, or treating supplements as less important than the personal statement, can derail an otherwise strong application. Ivy League supplements often ask specific questions about fit and contribution that deserve as much care and specificity as the main essay.

source_name: Ivy League Admissions Discussions - Widely reported in A2C and College Confidential communities
text:
Source:
Ivy League Admissions Discussions - Widely reported in A2C and College Confidential communities

School:
Multiple

Advice:
A concern that surfaces repeatedly in admissions discussions is students who apply to Ivy League schools without applying to any realistic safety schools. The mentality that one of the eight schools must surely accept you is a dangerous one. Students who apply exclusively to elite institutions and receive nothing but rejections have no backup, and all the time invested in those applications produces nothing actionable. A related mistake is applying to schools solely because of prestige and rankings without doing any actual research on whether the school is the right fit academically, socially, or geographically.

Key Takeaway:
A college list with no financial or academic safety schools is a gamble that frequently backfires. Applying to Ivy League schools should never crowd out thoughtful applications to schools where you are likely to be admitted. Building a balanced list is itself a skill and an important part of application strategy.

source_name: Ivy Coach Blog (ivycoach.com) - Commentary from former Ivy admissions readers
text:
Source:
Ivy Coach Blog (ivycoach.com) - Commentary from former Ivy admissions readers
https://www.ivycoach.com/the-ivy-coach-blog/college-admissions/college-admission-essays/

School:
Multiple Ivy League

Advice:
Former admissions readers for Ivy League schools described the specific mistake of bragging in the personal statement. Applicants who structured their essays as a list of accomplishments, framed as proof that they deserve admission, produced essays that admissions readers found unappealing. The essay is not a resume in paragraph form. Starting most sentences with I and cataloguing achievements without reflection or depth tends to come across as selling rather than sharing. One counselor put it directly: bragging is a reliable way to make an admissions officer want to recommend denial.

Key Takeaway:
The personal statement is not the place to list your accomplishments; your resume and activities section do that. An essay that reads as a sales pitch for why the admissions office should admit you misunderstands the purpose of the essay entirely. Reflection, vulnerability, and specificity are more effective than achievement summaries.

Each entry is sourced from a publicly available post, video, or blog. All advice is paraphrased or summarized from the original sources.
source_name: Admissions Decisions Substack (admissionsdecisions.substack.com) - Observations from students and counselors
text:
Source:
Admissions Decisions Substack (admissionsdecisions.substack.com) - Observations from students and counselors
https://admissionsdecisions.substack.com/p/5-more-things-to-avoid-in-college

School:
Multiple

Advice:
Two technical mistakes that appear in discussions consistently are failing to proofread essays and writing a response to an essay prompt that does not actually answer the question asked. Some students write a general essay they like and then look for a prompt it loosely fits, rather than writing to the specific prompt. Admissions readers notice this immediately. Similarly, typos and grammatical errors undermine an otherwise polished application. Spellcheck catches some errors but does not catch homophones, missing words, or sentences that are grammatically correct but logically incomplete.

Key Takeaway:
Writing a general essay and hunting for a prompt it fits is the wrong approach and results in essays that often do not address the question. Read each prompt carefully and write specifically to it. Proofread every essay multiple times and have at least two other people read for errors before submission.

source_name: College Confidential / A2C Community Discussions - Widely repeated advice from admitted students
text:
Source:
College Confidential / A2C Community Discussions - Widely repeated advice from admitted students

School:
Multiple Ivy League

Advice:
A recurring theme in admissions discussions from students who were admitted to Ivy League schools is the misconception that prestigious summer programs, such as paid university programs or expensive pre-college experiences, automatically boost an application. Multiple admitted students noted that these programs are often treated by admissions officers as resume padding unless the student can articulate specifically what they learned, how it influenced their thinking, or how it connected to their authentic interests. A student who attended a Harvard summer program and writes about it as a credential is less compelling than a student who spent that summer on a self-directed project that grew naturally from their interests.

Key Takeaway:
Expensive or prestigious-sounding summer programs do not carry the weight many applicants believe they do. Admissions readers are experienced at distinguishing between students who pursued experiences out of genuine interest and students whose parents enrolled them in programs to manufacture a credential. Authenticity of motivation matters more than the prestige of the activity.

source_name: Harvard College Student Stories Blog (college.harvard.edu) - Admitted student reflection
text:
Source:
Harvard College Student Stories Blog (college.harvard.edu) - Admitted student reflection
https://college.harvard.edu/student-life/student-stories/what-happened-when-i-got-accepted-harvard

School:
Harvard

Advice:
A Harvard student reflected honestly on the experience of worrying that they had been admitted by mistake and that attending would expose them as a fraud. This experience, often called impostor syndrome, is very common among admitted Ivy League students and suggests something important about the application process: the students who do get in often do not fit the fantasy image of a perfect, polished, supremely confident applicant. The mistake many applicants make is performing confidence and perfection rather than showing genuine curiosity, uncertainty, and growth. Admissions officers are reading for intellectual engagement and human dimension, not a flawless presentation.

Key Takeaway:
Applicants who craft a self-image of perfect achievement and certainty in their essays often come across as less compelling than those who show genuine intellectual curiosity and the capacity for growth. The admissions process rewards humans, not performances. Trying to appear perfect can make you seem less interesting than being honestly yourself.

source_name: Harvard Gazette, "How I Wrote My Harvard Essay" (September 6, 2019)
text:
Source:
Harvard Gazette, "How I Wrote My Harvard Essay" (September 6, 2019)
https://news.harvard.edu/gazette/story/2019/09/how-they-wrote-and-rewrote-their-harvard-admissions-essays/

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Allison Tu, a first-year student from Louisville, Kentucky, described her process as deliberately uninhibited. She stayed up late at night specifically because her lowered inhibitions allowed her to brainstorm without self-criticism. She generated approximately twenty ideas, narrowed them down to five, wrote full drafts of each of those five, and then chose one to revise repeatedly until it felt complete. Her final essay explored the transition from personal independence to interdependence, using her parents' divorce as the catalyst for that growth. The essay was not about the divorce itself but about what she learned from it: how to collaborate, lead others, and depend on a team to accomplish meaningful things in her community.

Key Takeaway:
Removing your internal critic during the brainstorming phase allows for more honest self-reflection. Generating many ideas before selecting one leads to a stronger final essay. Personal growth stories do not need to center on trauma but rather on the insight gained from it.

source_name: Harvard Gazette, "How I Wrote My Harvard Essay" (September 6, 2019)
text:
Source:
Harvard Gazette, "How I Wrote My Harvard Essay" (September 6, 2019)
https://news.harvard.edu/gazette/story/2019/09/how-they-wrote-and-rewrote-their-harvard-admissions-essays/

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Nick Nocita, from Arlington Heights, Illinois, wrote his Harvard essay about his grandmother, who passed away several years before he applied. He was struck by the inspiration mid-family gathering on Thanksgiving and wrote much of his essay on his phone while his family watched football. His grandmother had only completed a high school education, and seeing what she accomplished within those constraints made him think deeply about what he could do with the opportunity of a prestigious college education. The connection was personal and specific: he was not writing generically about loss but about how one person's limitations reframed his own sense of possibility and ambition.

Key Takeaway:
Essays that emerge from genuine emotion and personal connection, even in unexpected moments, often feel the most authentic. Writing about the influence of a family member works best when the reflection focuses on what the writer learned rather than simply describing the person.

source_name: Harvard Gazette, "How I Wrote My Harvard Essay" (September 6, 2019)
text:
Source:
Harvard Gazette, "How I Wrote My Harvard Essay" (September 6, 2019)
https://news.harvard.edu/gazette/story/2019/09/how-they-wrote-and-rewrote-their-harvard-admissions-essays/

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Divya Amirtharaj, from Portland, Oregon, described struggling for weeks with writer's block before finding her subject. Once she identified her topic, however, she completed her draft within a day. One of her essays focused on her identity as an Indian woman growing up in a predominantly white community, shaped in part by a visible skin condition called vitiligo. A second essay traced her evolution from competitive swimmer to lifeguard to swim instructor to founder of a free swimming program for underprivileged children in her area. She noted that looking back over what she had written felt like a condensed autobiography of her first seventeen years.

Key Takeaway:
Writer's block is normal; the breakthrough often comes from identifying a subject that genuinely captures your personal journey. Two very different essays can coexist in an application, each revealing a distinct and important dimension of who you are.

source_name: Harvard Gazette, "How I Wrote My Harvard Essay" (September 6, 2019)
text:
Source:
Harvard Gazette, "How I Wrote My Harvard Essay" (September 6, 2019)
https://news.harvard.edu/gazette/story/2019/09/how-they-wrote-and-rewrote-their-harvard-admissions-essays/

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Sophie Clivio, originally from Kingston, Jamaica, took an unconventional structural approach: she wrote her essay as a spoken-word poem rather than a traditional paragraph-based essay. Her subject was the cultural and racial identity shift she experienced when moving from Jamaica to attend boarding school in Milton, Massachusetts. She acknowledged later discovering she had submitted the essay with a typo and an undeleted editorial comment at the bottom. Despite the mistake, she was admitted. Her advice, implicitly, was that an authentic and distinctive format can be more powerful than a polished but generic one.

Key Takeaway:
Format experimentation can pay off if it reflects your genuine voice and identity. Admissions officers read thousands of traditional essays; one with a distinctive structure and authentic cultural perspective stands out. Perfection in formatting matters less than authenticity in content.

source_name:
Harvard College Student Blog, "A Senior's Advice on Applying to College"
Braeden, Class of 2021 Alumni, published December 2020
text:
Source:
Harvard College Student Blog, "A Senior's Advice on Applying to College"
Braeden, Class of 2021 Alumni, published December 2020
https://college.harvard.edu/life-at-harvard/student-stories/seniors-advice-applying-college

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Braeden, a Harvard senior who worked with the Harvard First Generation Program, shared that he believed essays were the single most important component of the application because they were the most direct opportunity to set yourself apart. He warned against participating in clubs simply to fill a resume, noting that a lack of genuine passion reveals itself in the application. In his own high school experience, he cared deeply about school clubs and mentorship, and had no massive award portfolio. He stressed the power of a strong personal narrative and recommended thinking about what experiences defined who you are rather than what you think the admissions committee wants to read.

Key Takeaway:
Essays are where applicants distinguish themselves most directly. Genuine engagement in activities matters more than trophy-hunting. Writing about what actually shaped you, rather than what sounds impressive, produces stronger results.

source_name:
Harvard College Student Blog, "Words of Encouragement for High School Students"
Ana, Class of 2025 Alumni, published December 2024
text:
Source:
Harvard College Student Blog, "Words of Encouragement for High School Students"
Ana, Class of 2025 Alumni, published December 2024
https://college.harvard.edu/student-life/student-stories/words-encouragement-high-school-students

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Ana, a Harvard senior who grew up never imagining she would attend an Ivy League school, described Harvard's admissions process as a "whole-person review." She noted that test scores and GPA are considered alongside recommendation letters, personal statements, and extracurricular activities. She encouraged applicants not to count themselves out because one element of their application is not perfect. Her most direct advice was about essays: she encouraged prospective students to use the personal statement as a space to tell the truth about who they are, not to construct a version of themselves they think will appeal to admissions officers.

Key Takeaway:
Harvard's admissions process evaluates all elements of an application together. No single weakness is necessarily disqualifying. The essay should reflect your real self, not a curated performance of who you think they want to admit.

source_name: Harvard College Student Blog, "A First Year's Perspective on Applying to Harvard"
text:
Source:
Harvard College Student Blog, "A First Year's Perspective on Applying to Harvard"
https://college.harvard.edu/student-life/student-stories/first-years-perspective-applying-harvard

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
A Harvard first-year student recommended starting the application process early, specifically getting the administrative and logistical pieces out of the way before the application season rush, so that focus could then shift entirely to essays and written responses. The student stressed getting feedback on personal and supplemental essays from at least one trusted reader, noting that what a writer thinks they are conveying may land differently with an outside reader. However, they cautioned against asking too many people for feedback, as this can lead to a diluted, committee-written essay that no longer sounds like the applicant.

Key Takeaway:
Starting early frees up time and mental energy for the essays, which matter most. One or two trusted readers give useful perspective without overwriting your voice. The essay must still sound like you.

source_name:
Princeton Admission Student Blog, "On Applying to College"
Lake Liao, Class of 2027, published September 5, 2024
text:
Source:
Princeton Admission Student Blog, "On Applying to College"
Lake Liao, Class of 2027, published September 5, 2024
https://admission.princeton.edu/blogs/applying-college

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
Lake Liao, a Princeton student concentrating in Philosophy, shared five principles that guided their successful application in fall 2022. The first was to know who you are, explaining that college applications require you to articulate a sense of self, and if you can answer questions like what you believe most deeply and what you can talk about for hours without preparation, those answers will translate into a compelling personal narrative. The second was to do your homework about the school, emphasizing that specific and genuine knowledge of a university's professors, research centers, student groups, and academic culture makes a far stronger impression than generic praise of the institution.

Key Takeaway:
Self-knowledge is the foundation of a strong application essay. The more clearly you understand what drives you, the more compelling your writing will be. School-specific research is not optional; it demonstrates genuine interest and seriousness.

source_name: Princeton Admission Office, "Helpful Tips for Applicants"
text:
Source:
Princeton Admission Office, "Helpful Tips for Applicants"
https://admission.princeton.edu/apply/before-you-apply/helpful-tips

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
Princeton's official guidance, contributed to by enrolled students and admissions staff, advises applicants to create an application that reveals their achievements in their true context rather than trying to meet a specific checklist. The advice emphasizes showing how you have taken advantage of what your high school offered and how you have contributed within your own particular environment. Princeton is clear that it looks for students who make a difference in their schools and communities, and the essay is where that difference gets articulated. Students are encouraged to describe leadership activities, special skills, job experiences, and even responsibilities at home, as these help contextualize the full picture of who the applicant is.

Key Takeaway:
The essay is an opportunity to frame your accomplishments in context, not to brag about them in isolation. Princeton wants to understand who you are within your own environment, not just how you compare to an abstract ideal applicant.

source_name: Yale University Admissions Office, "Advice on Putting Together Your Application"
text:
Source:
Yale University Admissions Office, "Advice on Putting Together Your Application"
https://admissions.yale.edu/advice-putting-together-your-application

School:
Yale University

Advice:
Yale's guidance, rooted in feedback from its admissions committee, encourages students to write about something that matters to them in their own voice. The office explicitly discourages students from using overly complex vocabulary or convoluted sentence structures to sound impressive. Yale notes that it has read strong essays on completely ordinary topics and weak essays on highly unusual ones, concluding that the perspective and lens through which a student views their topic is far more important than the topic itself. Yale specifically says it does not expect students to fit their entire life story into two short essays; rather, it wants essays that give a real sense of who the person is.

Key Takeaway:
Topic selection matters less than perspective and voice. Impressive vocabulary does not substitute for genuine insight. Yale wants to hear you, not the version of you that you think sounds most impressive.

source_name: Yale Admissions Officer Advice (via Veritas Essays admissions blog, citing Inside the Yale Admissions Office podcast)
text:
Source:
Yale Admissions Officer Advice (via Veritas Essays admissions blog, citing Inside the Yale Admissions Office podcast)
https://veritasessays.org/college-admissions-blog/posts/college-admissions-officers-best-college-essay-strategies-for-acceptance

School:
Yale University

Advice:
Yale admissions officers described the role of the personal essay as a kind of glue that binds together the other parts of the application. Rather than functioning as its own isolated showcase, the best essays create coherence across the entire application, making the admissions reader feel they are encountering the same person consistently throughout. This means the essay should connect to and reinforce what appears in the activity list, the recommendations, and the academic record. An essay that introduces a completely disconnected aspect of the applicant without threading back to the rest of the application loses this cohesive power.

Key Takeaway:
Think of the essay as a connective thread, not a standalone performance. When an admissions reader finishes your application and feels they have met one consistent person across every section, that is when the essay has done its job.

School:
Yale University, University of Pennsylvania, Dartmouth College

Advice:
Ohanna, a first-generation student who applied to 27 colleges and used every free advising resource available, wrote her personal statement about serving as her family's unofficial secretary growing up, handling translations, bills, and communications between her non-English-speaking parents and the outside world. This role, often invisible to others, was deeply formative. Her essay did not focus on hardship as a source of sympathy but as a source of skill: she had developed a particular comfort with navigating complexity and advocating for others. A Penn admissions officer noted in writing that her community prompt response was especially powerful. She attributed her acceptances in part to learning to push herself outside her comfort zone and to making her growth explicit in her writing.

Key Takeaway:
Everyday family responsibilities that might feel unremarkable can become powerful essay topics when framed around what they taught you. First-generation students should not downplay the complexity of their backgrounds; that complexity is part of what makes the narrative distinctive.

School:
Yale, Columbia, UPenn, Dartmouth, Cornell

Advice:
One student's successful essay used a trip to Costco as its central image and framing device. The student described navigating aisles as a child, using the experience to demonstrate genuine curiosity about human behavior, consumerism, and the nature of choice. The essay worked not because Costco is an interesting topic but because the student used it as a lens to reveal advanced thinking about human dynamics. The essay also successfully incorporated humor and wit, communicating personality alongside intellectual engagement. The admissions consultant analyzing the essay noted that this approach showed the student's curiosity and understanding of the world without ever listing achievements or sounding boastful.

Key Takeaway:
An ordinary, even mundane setting can anchor a powerful essay if you use it to reveal something genuine and distinctive about how you see the world. Humor, when deployed skillfully, can communicate personality in a way that formal writing cannot.

Source:

Essay by Erica, Princeton University Class of 2020
https://prepmaven.com/blog/applying/college-essay-examples/

School:
Princeton University (also accepted to Harvard, Williams College, Duke, Johns Hopkins, and others)

Advice:
Erica opened her Princeton Common Application personal statement with a fictional version of herself: at age eleven, she imagined having written a New York Times bestselling fantasy trilogy more popular than Harry Potter. She had just received a call from her parents about an amateur writing contest and immediately began drafting a manuscript. The essay used this humorous, self-aware opening to lead into a genuine reflection on her relationship with writing, ambition, and the distance between imagination and reality. The approach worked because it immediately established voice and personality, giving the admissions reader a vivid first impression before settling into more serious reflection. The essay prompt asked her to reflect on a time she challenged a belief or idea.

Key Takeaway:
A strong, confident opening that immediately establishes your voice is one of the most effective tools in an essay. Self-awareness and humor in your opening can draw a reader in before you transition to deeper reflection. Starting with a vivid, even slightly absurd image can be far more memorable than a traditional introduction.

School:
Ivy League (general, school not specified in public source)

Advice:
The consulting firm's analysis of multiple successful Ivy League applicants found that the strongest essays were written about subjects the student genuinely found interesting, regardless of whether those subjects seemed conventionally impressive. One profiled student, Camille, wrote about environmental issues, but the key was that she could write with conviction, excitement, and specificity. Students were advised to ask themselves: what does my interest in this subject say about me as a person? Working backwards from a character trait or personality quality toward an essay topic often produces more authentic results than starting with what seems like a strong topic and trying to fit yourself into it.

Key Takeaway:
Start with who you are, not with what sounds impressive. Authenticity, specificity, and genuine enthusiasm for a subject produce stronger essays than selecting a topic you think will appeal to an admissions committee. Ask what your chosen subject reveals about your character.

source_name: Cornell University Undergraduate Admissions, "Writing Your Application Essays for Cornell"
text:
Source:
Cornell University Undergraduate Admissions, "Writing Your Application Essays for Cornell"
https://admissions.cornell.edu/community/blog/writing-your-application-essays-for-cornell

School:
Cornell University

Advice:
Cornell's admissions guidance, reflecting the perspective of enrolled students and admissions staff, advises applicants to make their Cornell essays deeply personal. Students should think carefully about what has genuinely shaped their interests and why Cornell specifically is the right environment for their academic and professional goals. Cornell emphasizes that it reviews applications by college or school, meaning the school-specific supplemental essay must speak directly to the resources, programs, and community of the particular school you are applying to, not just Cornell as a brand. Students are encouraged to explore virtual visit resources, departmental pages, and faculty profiles before writing, so their essays reflect real knowledge rather than general impressions.

Key Takeaway:
For Cornell specifically, the supplemental essay must demonstrate meaningful knowledge of the specific college you are applying to within the university. Generic praise of Cornell will not be effective. The essay should explain why that particular school's resources align with your particular goals.

source_name: Cornell University, "Preparing for Your Cornell Application"
text:
Source:
Cornell University, "Preparing for Your Cornell Application"
https://admissions.cornell.edu/how-to-apply/preparing-for-your-cornell-application

School:
Cornell University

Advice:
Cornell's official preparation guidance lists the qualities it values most in applicants: character, honesty, open-mindedness, initiative, collaboration, empathy, and curiosity. These values are explicitly mentioned as things admissions officers look for in application essays and recommendations. Cornell also emphasizes that involvement, community engagement, extracurricular activities, work experience, research, leadership, and even family responsibilities all constitute legitimate subjects for the essay section. Students should ask themselves how they plan to take advantage of Cornell's learning environment and make clear why Cornell is the right place for them specifically.

Key Takeaway:
Cornell values character and curiosity above credentials. Family responsibilities and work experience are legitimate essay subjects, not lesser alternatives to academic awards. The essay should always answer the implicit question: why Cornell, and why you?

source_name:

Annotated examples from admitted students across all eight Ivies
text:
Source:
CollegeVine Blog, "8 Strong Ivy League Essay Examples"
Annotated examples from admitted students across all eight Ivies
https://blog.collegevine.com/ivy-league-essay-examples

School:
Yale University (example student), multiple Ivies (general analysis)

Advice:
Analysis of strong Yale essays found that the most effective ones did not use the supplemental as an opportunity to repeat accomplishments that already appeared elsewhere in the application. Admissions officers already see those achievements in the activity list and transcript. The essay is most powerful when it reveals something not visible anywhere else. One Yale-admitted student's essay opened with a vivid description of conditions at an overcrowded border detention facility, using that image to anchor an argument about immigration policy that connected directly to her intended academic path in public policy. The analysis also noted that extraneous details, even if impressive, can dilute an essay's impact when they do not connect to the central narrative.

Key Takeaway:
Do not repeat your resume in your essay. Use the essay to show something new. Every detail in the essay should serve the central narrative; impressive details that do not connect to the essay's argument should be cut.

text:
Source:
CollegeAdvisor.com, "Ivy League Essay Examples" (analysis of Yale-admitted student essay)
https://www.collegeadvisor.com/essay-guides/ivy-league-essay-examples/

School:
Yale University

Advice:
One Yale-admitted student wrote her personal statement about her mother's diagnosis with schizoaffective disorder. The essay dropped the reader directly into the moment the writer received a phone call from her grandmother about her mother's hospitalization, then backed up to give context. The essay was praised for its self-reflection and vulnerability, demonstrating that personal growth essays are among the most consistently effective approaches to the personal statement. What made the essay work was not the subject itself but the arc of understanding: the writer moved from hurt and anger to a transformed way of seeing the world, demonstrating genuine maturity and introspection.

Key Takeaway:
Vulnerability in an essay is a strength, not a weakness, when paired with genuine reflection and growth. The essay's emotional power comes not from the event described but from what the writer learned about themselves and the world as a result.

Essay accepted to University of Pennsylvania (UPenn)
text:
Source:

Essay accepted to University of Pennsylvania (UPenn)
https://www.shemmassianconsulting.com/blog/college-essay-examples

School:
University of Pennsylvania

Advice:
One UPenn-admitted student wrote about contracting yellow fever at age thirteen while visiting Liberia. Because the local doctor lacked access to diagnostic blood tests, a serious but treatable illness nearly became fatal. The student survived, and the experience ignited a sustained interest in global health disparities. The student went on to found the first high school branch of the African Disease Prevention Project. The essay worked because it connected a visceral, personal experience to a clear intellectual and action-oriented trajectory. The admissions analysis noted that the essay revealed both the student's authentic story and a compelling vision for how they would contribute to Penn's community.

Key Takeaway:
A personal experience becomes the strongest foundation for an essay when it connects directly to a sustained intellectual or practical commitment. The essay should trace a thread from who you were to who you became and why the school you are applying to is the right next step.

text:
Source:
College Essay Guy, "How to Write the Columbia University Supplemental Essays: Examples + Guide"
https://www.collegeessayguy.com/blog/columbia-supplemental-essay

School:
Columbia University

Advice:
Analysis of successful Columbia essays found that the most effective ones did not reach for obviously prestigious or political topics. Columbia's supplemental prompts about books, music, films, and conversation topics are specifically designed to reveal personality and intellectual character. Students who chose obscure, personal, or unexpected topics were often more memorable than those who reached for the most serious or socially important subject available. One student effectively used the contrast between Tiger Balm and Advil as a framing device to explore cultural difference and his approach to debate. The essay worked because it was specific, personal, and revealed character in a way that a global policy topic could not.

Key Takeaway:
For Columbia's supplementals especially, smaller and more personal topics often outperform large, obvious ones. Choose subjects that reveal how you think, not subjects that signal what you think you should care about.

University of Pennsylvania admissions guidance and CollegeEssayAdvisors analysis
https://www.collegeessayadvisors.com/supplemental-essay/university-of-pennsylvania-upenn-supplemental-essay-prompt-guide/

School:
University of Pennsylvania

Advice:
UPenn's supplemental essays are designed to reveal how a student thinks about their future and how they would participate in the Penn community once admitted. Advisors who have worked with admitted Penn students note that the strongest essays do not just describe goals in the abstract but connect those goals to specific Penn resources: a named club, a research center, a particular faculty member's work, or a campus organization. Penn is described as seeking students who are curious about everything and drawn to other people, and the essay should reflect both qualities through story and specific detail.

Key Takeaway:
UPenn supplementals need to demonstrate genuine knowledge of Penn-specific resources and communities, not just enthusiasm for Penn as a name brand. Stories drawn from real life, tied to specific campus opportunities, are consistently more effective than abstract statements of ambition.

source_name: IvyWise, "Crafting a Winning Why Dartmouth Essay: Tips and Tricks"
text:
Source:
IvyWise, "Crafting a Winning Why Dartmouth Essay: Tips and Tricks"
https://www.ivywise.com/blog/dartmouth-essay/

School:
Dartmouth College

Advice:
Dartmouth's supplemental essay prompts, including the short "Why Dartmouth?" essay capped at one hundred words, require students to identify specific aspects of Dartmouth's academic programs, community, and campus environment that attract them. Advisors who have worked with admitted Dartmouth students emphasize that with so few words, every word must do specific work. Generic statements about Dartmouth's prestige or beautiful campus are wasted opportunities. The most effective responses identify a specific program, a distinctive Dartmouth tradition, a faculty research initiative, or a student organization that connects meaningfully to the applicant's demonstrated interests.

Key Takeaway:
Word limits force discipline. In a hundred-word supplemental, specificity is everything. Do not describe what Dartmouth is; describe what Dartmouth has that you want and why your background makes you ready for it.

Source:
Admissions Decisions Substack, "Success Story: Accepted to Harvard"
https://admissionsdecisions.substack.com/p/success-story-accepted-to-harvard

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
A profile of a Harvard early action admit described a student with a perfect GPA, a 36 ACT, thirteen AP classes, projected valedictorian standing, strong letters of recommendation, and essays begun more than a month before the deadline. The profile noted that the essay quality was specifically highlighted as "strong" and directly tied to the early start. The analyst emphasized that for highly competitive applicants, the essay becomes the differentiating factor because so many other parts of the application look similar. Starting the essay process over a month before the deadline allowed for genuine revision and reflection rather than a rushed draft.

Key Takeaway:
At Harvard's level of competition, many applicants have nearly identical academic profiles. The essay is often the primary differentiating factor. Starting early enough to genuinely revise makes a measurable difference in essay quality.

School:
Cornell University, Dartmouth College, Yale University, Princeton University

Advice:
One student who was accepted to all ten schools he applied to, including Cornell, Dartmouth, Yale, and Princeton, wrote an essay that was noted for demonstrating exceptional intellectual engagement with a subject outside of his formal academic record. The consultant's analysis highlighted that top Ivy League schools want to see evidence of independent thinking and genuine curiosity, not just a record of accomplishments. The essay revealed who the student was as a thinker, not just as an achiever. This distinction, between demonstrating what you have done versus who you are when you engage with the world, was identified as the central factor that separated strong essays from exceptional ones.

Key Takeaway:
Demonstrating how you think is more powerful than cataloguing what you have done. The strongest essays reveal a distinct intellectual personality. Admissions officers at the most selective schools are looking for thinkers, not just achievers.

source_name: Karina Macosko, interview published by AcademicInfluence.com, "What Helped Me Get Into Princeton" (Updated July 28, 2023)
text:
Source:
Karina Macosko, interview published by AcademicInfluence.com, "What Helped Me Get Into Princeton" (Updated July 28, 2023)
https://academicinfluence.com/interviews/college-prep/what-helped-me-get-into-princeton

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
Karina Macosko, who was admitted to Princeton early decision with a 4.7 GPA and as the top student in her class, offered a clear warning to underclassmen: do not join activities to pad a resume. She explicitly called this the biggest myth she encountered in high school. She noted that admissions officers can tell when a student has done something for one year and dropped it, reading that pattern as resume-building rather than genuine passion. Her own extracurriculars grew from real interest: she was on varsity track for all four years of high school, played volleyball for two years, and danced for two years outside of school. She never planned on running track in college but loved it in high school. She stressed that the application will naturally reflect your passions if you pursue things you actually care about, and that trying to manufacture an impressive list will backfire.

Key Takeaway:
Do not join activities for how they look on an application. Admissions officers are trained to spot the difference between genuine commitment and strategic participation. Sustained involvement in something you genuinely enjoy will always be more compelling than a curated list of impressive-sounding clubs you barely attended.

Harvard College Student Blog, "A Senior's Advice on Applying to College"
Braeden, Class of 2021 Alumni, published December 2020
text:
Source:
Harvard College Student Blog, "A Senior's Advice on Applying to College"
Braeden, Class of 2021 Alumni, published December 2020
https://college.harvard.edu/life-at-harvard/student-stories/seniors-advice-applying-college

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Braeden, who graduated Harvard and spent over three years working with the Harvard First Generation Program, reflected that his extracurricular profile was built on things he actually cared about rather than on credentials. He cared deeply about his school clubs and about mentorship. He had not accumulated a long list of awards or claims to fame. But he consistently poured genuine energy into what he did. He observed that high school students often feel enormous pressure to pursue the most impressive-looking activities, even when those activities hold no personal meaning. He argued that a lack of genuine passion reveals itself in the application because it shows up in the essays, in the descriptions, and even in the recommendations. What matters most, in his view, is that you care about what you are doing, not that it looks prestigious from the outside.

Key Takeaway:
Being a national award winner is not a prerequisite for Harvard admission. Demonstrating genuine commitment and investment in activities you care about is more impressive than collecting trophies in activities that bore you. Authentic engagement shows up across the entire application.

Ana, Class of 2025 Alumni, published December 2024
text:
Source:
Harvard College Student Blog, "Words of Encouragement for High School Students"
Ana, Class of 2025 Alumni, published December 2024
https://college.harvard.edu/student-life/student-stories/words-encouragement-high-school-students

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Ana, a Harvard senior and first-generation college student, described having felt intimidated by lists of extracurriculars she saw other students discuss online. She felt that unless she was doing ten or more incredible activities in high school, she would not be competitive at selective schools. Learning about Harvard's whole-person review process changed that perception. She came to understand that Harvard does not evaluate activities in isolation but within the context of what opportunities the student actually had access to. A student who helped care for siblings, held a part-time job, and participated in a couple of school clubs would be evaluated differently than a student from a wealthy school with access to dozens of funded programs. Her advice was to get involved in some extracurricular opportunities, work hard in classes, and trust that effort across the whole application adds up.

Key Takeaway:
Harvard evaluates extracurriculars in the context of what opportunities were realistically available to you. Students without access to expensive programs or elite summer opportunities are not at an inherent disadvantage if they show commitment and genuine engagement with what they did have access to.

source_name: Harvard College Alumni Profile, "Precious '07" (former Harvard admissions officer and Harvard varsity fencing athlete)
text:
Source:
Harvard College Alumni Profile, "Precious '07" (former Harvard admissions officer and Harvard varsity fencing athlete)
https://college.harvard.edu/admissions/why-harvard/alumni/precious-nwankwo-07

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Precious Nwankwo, a Harvard alumna who went on to serve as a Harvard admissions officer before becoming a lawyer, reflected that her extracurricular activities formed the richest part of her Harvard experience. In high school and in college, she was a member of the Varsity Fencing Team for all four years, worked at Widener Library, served as a research assistant at the Kennedy School, and acted as a student coordinator for the Harvard Financial Aid Initiative. As a former admissions officer, she offered this perspective: the college application process, as challenging as it feels, is fundamentally an exercise in introspection. Extracurriculars are not just items on a list; they are evidence of how you spend your time and what you value. She knew from the admissions side how much deliberation goes into each class selection, and from the student side how much those choices shaped who she became at Harvard.

Key Takeaway:
The extracurriculars you pursue in high school should genuinely reflect your values and interests, because those same activities will shape your experience in college. A former Harvard admissions officer confirmed that the committee evaluates activities as evidence of what you care about, not just what you have done.

source_name: Harvard College Application Tips (official admissions guidance)
text:
Source:
Harvard College Application Tips (official admissions guidance)
https://college.harvard.edu/guides/application-tips

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Harvard's official guidance on the extracurricular section explicitly acknowledges that not every student has access to a wide range of activities. Some high schools offer many funded clubs and sports programs; others offer almost none. Harvard's admissions committee takes this into account when reading the activities list. The guidance advises students to list the activities they care most about and devote the most time to, rather than either listing every single thing they have ever done or selectively listing only what seems most impressive to an admissions officer. The committee also explicitly notes that some of the most meaningful activities are things that happen outside of school, including jobs, family responsibilities, and community involvement, and that these count fully as extracurriculars.

Key Takeaway:
Harvard does not penalize students for having limited extracurricular access. Family responsibilities, jobs, and community involvement count as legitimate and meaningful extracurricular activities. List what you actually care about most, not what you think will sound most impressive.

text:
Source:
Princeton University Official Admissions Tips
https://admission.princeton.edu/apply/before-you-apply/helpful-tips

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
Princeton's official application guidance states directly that most Princeton students were academic standouts in high school, but also invested their energy and talents in significant ways outside the classroom. Princeton wants to know what you care about, not just what you have achieved. The guidance encourages students to describe leadership activities, special skills, and extracurricular involvement, and to discuss any jobs or responsibilities they have held at home. This explicit inclusion of domestic responsibilities reflects Princeton's awareness that not all meaningful extracurricular involvement happens in school clubs or national competitions. The guidance also encourages students to show how they have taken advantage of what their specific high school had to offer, framing achievements in their proper context rather than comparing them to some abstract ideal.

Key Takeaway:
Princeton measures extracurricular engagement in context. What matters is whether you made the most of what was available to you and contributed meaningfully within your community, not whether you had access to the same opportunities as students at elite private schools.

source_name:
Princeton Admission Student Blog, "On Applying to College"
Lake Liao, Class of 2027, published September 5, 2024
text:
Source:
Princeton Admission Student Blog, "On Applying to College"
Lake Liao, Class of 2027, published September 5, 2024
https://admission.princeton.edu/blogs/applying-college

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
Lake Liao, a Princeton student from Troy, Michigan, who concentrated in Philosophy, shared that one of the most important things a prospective student can do before applying is genuinely know who they are. In the context of extracurriculars, this means being able to say what you are willing to talk about for hours without preparation, what you do with your free time when no one is watching, and what you would keep doing even if it had no application value at all. These questions, when answered honestly, naturally lead to the activities that will make the strongest impression on an application. Lake noted that trying to engineer an impressive extracurricular profile from the outside in rarely works, but that students who pursued things they genuinely loved tended to develop the kind of specific, interesting stories that make applications memorable.

Key Takeaway:
The most powerful extracurricular activities on an application are the ones you would have done anyway, regardless of whether they helped you get into college. Genuine self-knowledge is the foundation of a compelling activities section.

source_name:
Yale Extracurricular Activities case study, The Koppelman Group (TKG)
"Yale Extracurricular Activities for High School Students" (June 22, 2023)
text:
Source:
Yale Extracurricular Activities case study, The Koppelman Group (TKG)
"Yale Extracurricular Activities for High School Students" (June 22, 2023)
https://www.koppelmangroup.com/blog/2023/6/22/yale-extracurricular-activities-for-high-school-students

School:
Yale University

Advice:
Analysis of an admitted Yale student's extracurricular profile showed a pattern of following a single core passion to its highest possible level, then building outward from there. The student loved debate. She spent four years on her high school debate team, became co-captain, and her team reached the 8th national ranking during her time on it. She placed in the top 30 of the National Speech and Debate Tournament and won an NSDA All-American Award and NSDA Honor Society Premier Distinction. Beyond competition, she extended her passion into mentorship by teaching middle schoolers debate through a women in debate society. Separately, she pursued her academic interest in chemistry by setting up an independent research project at a local college lab, where she invented an infrared device capable of detecting GHB in drinks. She filed for a patent on the design, built a working prototype, and won first place at a nationally ranked science fair. The analysis emphasized that Yale wants students who excel at their passion and then deepen it beyond the obvious.

Key Takeaway:
For Yale, the model is not well-roundedness but mastery. Identify the thing you care most about, commit to it for multiple years, take on leadership within it, achieve at the highest level accessible to you, and then extend it into teaching, research, or creation. That arc of depth and impact is what Yale is consistently looking for.

source_name: Yale Admissions Office, "Advice on Putting Together Your Application"
text:
Source:
Yale Admissions Office, "Advice on Putting Together Your Application"
https://admissions.yale.edu/advice-putting-together-your-application

School:
Yale University

Advice:
Yale's official guidance on the extracurricular section states that the activities list should demonstrate several things simultaneously: that a student engages with community beyond the classroom, that they take leadership positions when available, and that they have invested meaningful energy into the activities they choose. Yale explicitly notes that a student does not need to be the president of a national organization to impress the admissions committee. What the committee wants to see is that the student has spent time pursuing meaningful opportunities and has had a positive impact on the people around them. Yale also emphasizes that it expects students will take advantage of the university's hundreds of student organizations, and the application should reflect someone who will actually do that, not just someone who joined clubs to fill a list.

Key Takeaway:
Yale does not require national prestige or top officer positions. It does require evidence of meaningful engagement, real impact on others, and a leadership orientation. The question is not what title you held but what difference you made.

source_name:
Brown University Extracurricular Activities case study, The Koppelman Group (TKG)
"Brown Extracurricular Activities" (June 30, 2023)
text:
Source:
Brown University Extracurricular Activities case study, The Koppelman Group (TKG)
"Brown Extracurricular Activities" (June 30, 2023)
https://www.koppelmangroup.com/blog/2023/6/30/brown-extracurricular-activities

School:
Brown University

Advice:
A case study of a student admitted to Brown detailed an unusually genuine and diverse extracurricular profile rooted in authentic interests rather than strategic selection. The student wanted to major in Classics and minor in Chemistry. To demonstrate those interests outside of class, he took a summer course in Hieroglyphics at Brown itself and taught himself Middle English so he could read Beowulf in the original language. He took online summer immersion courses at Columbia in Modern Chemistry and Materials Science. He also volunteered on weekends teaching children science through a Columbia University program. Outside of academic interests, he loved glassblowing, trained at a studio, and did public outreach workshops for them. He designed his own board game and launched it on Kickstarter. He co-founded a Naturalist Club at his high school as co-president and organized trips to local parks. He also cared about cancer research and co-presided over a fundraising group. The analysis concluded that Brown wants students who follow their unique passions with energy and who bring that same energy to campus.

Key Takeaway:
Brown values genuine intellectual curiosity and diverse authentic interests over prestige-optimized activities. You do not need to do community service or debate if those things do not reflect who you are. Follow what you actually love and pursue it seriously.

text:
Source:
Brown University Official Admissions Guidance (cited in TKG case study)
https://www.koppelmangroup.com/blog/2023/6/30/brown-extracurricular-activities

School:
Brown University

Advice:
Brown's own admissions language, cited in the case study analysis, states that it reviews the depth and breadth of academic learning that applicants have undertaken, which includes whether they have stretched themselves with outside-of-school educational opportunities. This framing is significant: Brown places pursuing academic interests outside the classroom on the same level as formal extracurricular activities. A student who independently reads primary sources in their field, takes online courses beyond school requirements, or sets up a self-directed project to explore an academic interest is showing exactly the kind of intellectual initiative Brown values. The university is explicitly looking for students who do not wait for a class to explore something that interests them.

Key Takeaway:
Brown evaluates outside-of-school educational initiatives as part of the extracurricular picture. Self-directed learning, independent research, and pursuing academic subjects beyond what school offers all count meaningfully and signal the intellectual curiosity Brown prizes.

school: Harvard University, Yale University, and other top schools (data-driven analysis)
source_name:
Collegebase.org, "Best Extracurricular Activities for College: What Top Schools Actually Want"
Data analysis of 1,100+ admitted student profiles at Harvard, Yale, Stanford, and other top schools (January 3, 2025)
text:
Source:
Collegebase.org, "Best Extracurricular Activities for College: What Top Schools Actually Want"
Data analysis of 1,100+ admitted student profiles at Harvard, Yale, Stanford, and other top schools (January 3, 2025)
https://www.collegebase.org/blog/best-extracurriculars-for-college

School:
Harvard University, Yale University, and other top schools (data-driven analysis)

Advice:
Analysis of over 1,100 admitted student profiles from Harvard, Yale, Stanford, MIT, and other top schools revealed that the conventional wisdom about being well-rounded is wrong. The data showed that admitted students averaged 5.2 activities total, with the most prominent single activity lasting an average of 3.5 years. The dominant pattern across admits was not a long list of varied activities but rather two to three deep, sustained commitments supported by a smaller number of secondary involvements. Students who had ten or more activities with shallow involvement were more commonly found in rejected applications than admitted ones. The analysis also identified that initiative consistently outweighed participation: students who founded, created, or launched something had a stronger profile than students who joined existing organizations. The clearest red flags identified were activities started only in junior or senior year, which read as resume padding; a list of activities that did not connect into a coherent narrative; and leadership titles that did not match the scale or nature of the activity described.

Key Takeaway:
The data from over 1,100 admitted students at top schools directly contradicts the myth of well-roundedness. Having two to three deeply committed, multi-year activities with leadership and measurable impact is far more effective than a long list of shallow involvements. Founding or creating something outperforms joining something every time.

school: Harvard University, Yale University (general top-school data)
source_name: Collegebase.org data analysis, continued
text:
Source:
Collegebase.org data analysis, continued
https://www.collegebase.org/blog/best-extracurriculars-for-college

School:
Harvard University, Yale University (general top-school data)

Advice:
The Collegebase analysis further identified specific activities that appear frequently in rejected applications and offer little differentiation value at the most selective schools. These include National Honor Society membership, which is near-universal among academically strong applicants and therefore does not distinguish any individual; generic hospital or community volunteering consisting of one-time or shallow service events; and resume-building programs like certain leadership conferences or statewide government programs when pursued without genuine interest. The analysis was clear that these activities are not harmful to an application, but they do not move the needle. The activities that differentiated admits from non-admits were those that showed the applicant had identified a problem, created something new, led others, or achieved meaningful measurable outcomes in an area they had been pursuing for years.

Key Takeaway:
National Honor Society, basic volunteering, and one-time leadership programs do not differentiate your application at the Ivy League level. What matters is multi-year commitment, self-initiated projects, measurable impact, and a coherent through-line that connects your activities to who you are.

source_name: Cornell University Admissions, "Preparing for Your Cornell Application"
text:
Source:
Cornell University Admissions, "Preparing for Your Cornell Application"
https://admissions.cornell.edu/how-to-apply/preparing-for-your-cornell-application

School:
Cornell University

Advice:
Cornell's official admissions preparation page identifies the qualities it values in extracurricular involvement: character, honesty, initiative, collaboration, empathy, and curiosity. Cornell also makes explicit that involvement, community engagement, extracurricular activities, work experience, research, leadership, and family responsibilities are all legitimate categories of engagement. Students applying to Cornell are encouraged to think about what they have spent time outside the classroom doing and to frame those activities around how they reflect the applicant's values. The guidance also tells students to think about how they plan to contribute to Cornell's community, making clear that the activities list is read not just as a record of the past but as a signal of what the student will do at Cornell.

Key Takeaway:
Cornell reads extracurriculars as evidence of values, not just accomplishments. Family responsibilities and work experience are explicitly valued alongside traditional club involvement. Frame your activities around what they reveal about your character and what you plan to bring to Cornell's community.

source_name: AdmitReport.com, "How to Get Into Cornell"
text:
Source:
AdmitReport.com, "How to Get Into Cornell"
https://admitreport.com/blog/how-to-get-into-cornell

School:
Cornell University

Advice:
Independent analysis of Cornell admissions concluded that Cornell cares less about what specific activities you have pursued than how you pursued them. The key is demonstrating that in whatever activities you chose, you aimed to be as excellent and impactful as possible. Even an activity as unconventional as regularly watching younger siblings while parents work is a legitimate and meaningful extracurricular, provided the student articulates the context and meaning behind it honestly. Cornell admissions officers evaluate the activities section for evidence of willingness to get involved and take action, a phrase the university uses repeatedly in its own communications. The activities section on the Common App goes to all schools equally, so Cornell admissions officers look at it knowing it was not tailored to them specifically, and they read between the lines for character and initiative.

Key Takeaway:
At Cornell, the quality of engagement within any activity matters more than the prestige of the activity itself. Even family responsibilities count, when articulated with context and honesty. Cornell is looking for students who take action and invest fully in whatever they choose to do.
