source_name: Dartmouth College Admissions, "My Why Dartmouth" (student reflection, 3D Magazine)
text:
Source:
Dartmouth College Admissions, "My Why Dartmouth" (student reflection, 3D Magazine)
https://admissions.dartmouth.edu/follow/3d-magazine/my-why-dartmouth-2

School:
Dartmouth College

Advice:
A Dartmouth student's personal reflection on why they chose Dartmouth offered a vivid window into what the kind of applicant Dartmouth attracts actually looks like on the ground. The student described a single day that moved from a research meeting with a climate science professor to scouting hiking trails as director of The Fifty, a 54-mile hiking challenge from Mount Moosilauke to Hanover, to dinner with leaders of First-Year Trips, to teaching friends to stitch leather into a wallet. This student had served as a search and rescue EMT, a wedding photographer, and vice president of their Greek house. They led Dartmouth Outing Club trips nationally and played guitar in a campus band. The reflection made clear that what attracted them to Dartmouth was not any single credential but the school's culture of multi-dimensional engagement, where students are deeply invested in radically different pursuits simultaneously and bring that same energy to every space they enter.

Key Takeaway:
Dartmouth explicitly values students who pursue radically different activities with equal seriousness and bring genuine energy to all of them. The student who is both a research EMT and a leather-stitching hobbyist and a trail director is exactly the kind of multi-dimensional person Dartmouth is built for.

text:
Source:
AdmitStudio.com, "How to Get Into Yale: What Actually Works"
https://admitstudio.com/undergraduate-articles/how-to-get-into-yale/

School:
Yale University

Advice:
Analysis of what consistently differentiates admitted Yale applicants identified several clear patterns in the extracurricular section. Rather than trying to appear well-rounded by joining many clubs, admitted students focused on two or three activities they genuinely cared about and committed to them deeply over multiple years. Leadership mattered significantly, and the analysis emphasized that formal titles matter less than demonstrated concrete impact. The questions that matter most when describing an extracurricular at Yale are: Did you expand a program? Did you secure funding? Did you train new members? Did you change school policy? Did you identify a problem and implement a solution? Yale specifically wants evidence that the applicant did not just participate but initiated change and contributed meaningfully. Describing hours spent is less valuable than describing what changed because of you.

Key Takeaway:
Yale evaluates extracurriculars by asking what changed because of your involvement. Hours spent and titles held matter less than concrete, describable outcomes. Expanding a program, training others, securing funding, or changing policy are exactly the kinds of impacts Yale is looking for.

text:
Source:
Harvard College Alumni Profile, "Sebastian '14" (Harvard Band, Harvard Yearbook, Southie After School Program volunteer)
https://college.harvard.edu/admissions/why-harvard/alumni/sebastian-14

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Sebastian, a Harvard alumnus, described his extracurricular involvement as centered on the Harvard University Band, the Harvard Yearbook, his residential house community organization, and consistent volunteer work with the Southie After School Program through Phillips Brooks House Association. Looking back, he described his activities as diverse but consistently oriented toward things he genuinely cared about: music, community memory-making, and service to young people in the local neighborhood. His reflection reinforced the pattern seen across Harvard's alumni profiles: the students who most valued their extracurricular involvement were those who chose activities for genuine connection rather than resume value. He also noted that it was through those activities that he met the people who became most important to his college experience.

Key Takeaway:
Extracurriculars at Harvard matter because of who you will meet through them and what you will build through sustained engagement. Students who chose activities for real connection consistently report that those activities defined their experience. The application is just the beginning.

source_name: Fortunaadmissions.com, "Best Extracurricular Activities for Ivy League Schools" (February 5, 2026)
text:
Source:
Fortunaadmissions.com, "Best Extracurricular Activities for Ivy League Schools" (February 5, 2026)
https://fortunaadmissions.com/college/best-extracurricular-activities-for-ivy-league-schools/

School:
All Ivy League schools (general)

Advice:
Admissions analysis from Fortuna Admissions, whose team includes former Ivy League admissions officers, identified the clearest single pattern across all eight Ivy League schools: the most powerful extracurricular activities are those where the student has shown progression over time, moving from participant to leader, from learner to mentor, or from curiosity to mastery. An activity that shows this arc, regardless of what the activity is, tells a more powerful story than a prestigious activity where the student simply showed up and did what was required. The progression arc matters because it demonstrates discipline, resilience, and the ability to set and pursue long-term goals. It also provides a natural narrative thread that connects different parts of the application together, including the essay and the recommendations.

Key Takeaway:
The single most powerful extracurricular narrative is an arc of growth: beginning as a participant and becoming a leader, mentor, or creator over multiple years. This arc demonstrates long-term goal-setting and signals the kind of engaged student every Ivy League school wants on campus.

source_name: Princeton University, President's Page, Princeton Alumni Weekly (October 22, 2003; cited as historical context for Ivy League EC norms)
text:
Source:
Princeton University, President's Page, Princeton Alumni Weekly (October 22, 2003; cited as historical context for Ivy League EC norms)
https://swh.princeton.edu/~paw/archive_new/PAW03-04/03-1022/prezpage.html

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
Princeton's president at the time described the prize-winning academic students on campus in a way that remains relevant as a model for what Ivy League schools have always valued: students who combine exceptional academic performance with passionate commitment to an extracurricular interest. The students highlighted were a member of the University Wind Ensemble and a co-captain of the ballroom dance team who also led a tap dancing group. Neither activity was conventionally prestigious in the way that debate nationals or science fair championships might be. What they signaled was deep, multi-year commitment to a genuine passion. Princeton's president quoted The Phantom Tollbooth to capture this ideal: my angles are many, my sides are not few, meaning Princeton has long valued students who are dimensionally interesting, not just measurably impressive.

Key Takeaway:
Princeton has historically valued students who combine academic excellence with passionate extracurricular investment, regardless of whether that investment is in a conventionally impressive activity. Deep commitment to any genuine passion reads clearly to admissions committees.

source_name:
Patch.com community post, "A Few Words of Advice on Extracurricular Involvement"
(College admissions counselor guidance widely shared in community forums)
text:
Source:
Patch.com community post, "A Few Words of Advice on Extracurricular Involvement"
(College admissions counselor guidance widely shared in community forums)
https://patch.com/minnesota/mendotaheights/a-few-words-of-advice-on-extracurricular-involvement_ae30da92

School:
All Ivy League schools (general)

Advice:
A widely circulated piece of guidance from college admissions counselors tackled the most common extracurricular misconception directly: that the goal is to accumulate as many activities as possible. The guidance identified that superficiality will not get students far and that college admissions officers are both experienced and perceptive enough to distinguish between genuine engagement and perfunctory participation. The guidance recommended focusing on depth over breadth, noting that students who assume leadership roles and participate extensively, spending ten to twenty hours per week in one or two pursuits, will consistently outshine students who dabble in many activities. The guidance also explicitly encouraged students who have unconventional interests: a student filmmaker or poetry club founder is just as intriguing to admissions committees as a student council president or varsity team captain, provided the commitment is deep and consistent.

Key Takeaway:
Breadth without depth does not impress Ivy League admissions committees. Students who spend ten to twenty hours per week over multiple years in one or two genuine pursuits consistently outperform students with a long list of shallow involvements. Unconventional activities are fully legitimate if the commitment is real.

source_name:
AdmittedlyHQ.com, "Ivy League Acceptance Rates 2024: Complete Analysis for the Class of 2028"
(Citing admissions patterns from Dartmouth, Harvard, and other Ivies)
text:
Source:
AdmittedlyHQ.com, "Ivy League Acceptance Rates 2024: Complete Analysis for the Class of 2028"
(Citing admissions patterns from Dartmouth, Harvard, and other Ivies)
https://admittedly.co/ivy-league-acceptance-rates-2024-complete-analysis/

School:
All Ivy League schools (general)

Advice:
Analysis of admissions patterns for the Class of 2028 cycle identified what admissions consultants and former officers called the spike model as the dominant framework for evaluating extracurriculars at elite schools. The spike refers to a coherent academic and extracurricular through-line that connects to specific faculty, labs, centers, or programs at target schools. Successful applicants were described as those who started building their spike early enough for it to compound: accumulating research experience, competition results, service leadership, or creative work over several years, rather than cramming impressive-looking activities into junior and senior year. The analysis specifically recommended identifying research, competition, service ventures, or original projects early in high school and deepening them over time, noting that these are the kinds of activities that can be connected to specific departments and programs during the application in a way that feels authentic rather than manufactured.

Key Takeaway:
The spike model, a coherent through-line connecting your main extracurricular passion to your academic interests and your target school's specific resources, is the most consistently effective extracurricular strategy at elite schools. It requires starting early so the spike compounds meaningfully by application time.

school: Harvard University, Yale University, Stanford University (data-driven)

Source:
Collegebase.org data analysis, section on activity tiers (January 3, 2025)
https://www.collegebase.org/blog/best-extracurriculars-for-college

School:
Harvard University, Yale University, Stanford University (data-driven)

Advice:
The Collegebase data identified a clear example of how depth and leadership outperform a long list even without Tier 1 national achievements. One Stanford admit in the database had only four activities: editor-in-chief of the school newspaper for four years, varsity cross country for four years, consistent volunteer work at a local food bank for three years, and a part-time job at a bookstore for two years. This student had no national competition wins, no published research, and no obvious spike. What they had was exceptional depth, demonstrated leadership, and multi-year sustained commitment across every activity they listed. The analysis used this example to make the point that students without exceptional credentials can still compete at elite schools if they demonstrate the arc of growth, leadership, and genuine commitment within whatever activities they pursue.

Key Takeaway:
You do not need national awards or research publications to build a competitive extracurricular profile. Sustained multi-year commitment with demonstrated leadership in a small number of activities is enough to compete at the highest level, even without Tier 1 achievements.

text:
Source:
Harvard College Official Guidance on Extracurricular Context (Application Tips page)

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Harvard's application tips page includes a specific acknowledgment that limited economic resources in many families can affect a student's ability to participate in school sports, travel teams, or certain activities due to equipment costs, logistical demands, and time constraints. Harvard's response to this reality is direct: students should not feel their chances of admission are diminished by a lack of extracurricular opportunities. The admissions committee explicitly commits to looking at the opportunities a student had available and assessing how well they took advantage of those opportunities within their actual circumstances. This framing means a student who worked twenty hours per week at a part-time job and could only join one school club is not in a worse position than a student with twelve activities, if the engagement within each context was genuine and meaningful.

Key Takeaway:
Harvard's admissions committee is explicitly trained to evaluate extracurriculars in context. Economic limitations, family obligations, and school resource constraints are all factored into how the activities list is read. What matters is the quality of engagement, not the quantity of opportunities.

source_name:
Princeton Tiger Life (student blog), "How to Find Community on Campus by Joining In"
Srista Tripathi, Class of 2025, published December 30, 2023
text:
Source:
Princeton Tiger Life (student blog), "How to Find Community on Campus by Joining In"
Srista Tripathi, Class of 2025, published December 30, 2023
https://tigerlife.princeton.edu/how-find-community-campus-joining

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
Srista Tripathi, a Princeton student who grew up as someone known for thriving through extracurricular activities, reflected on the process of maintaining and evolving her extracurricular life when she arrived at Princeton. She had been a dancer for fifteen years and knew she wanted to continue that in college. Her advice for prospective students was to think about what you want to do on campus before you arrive, because knowing your interests helps you identify the groups that will become your community. At Princeton, she noted, almost every activity exists as a club, and the range is extraordinary. The most important thing is figuring out, before you apply, what truly matters to you, because that clarity will make both your application and your actual college experience more coherent and meaningful. Students who could not answer the question of what they genuinely cared about in high school struggled to find community when they arrived.

Key Takeaway:
Knowing clearly what activities matter to you before you apply produces a more coherent application and a more meaningful college experience. Students who join activities in high school out of genuine interest arrive at college with a clear sense of where they want to spend their time and find community more easily.

source_name: Harvard College, "What to Expect After You Apply" (official admissions guidance)
text:
Source:
Harvard College, "What to Expect After You Apply" (official admissions guidance)
https://college.harvard.edu/guides/what-expect-after-you-apply

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Harvard's official guidance on post-application steps describes the interview as something that may or may not happen, based entirely on alumni availability and admissions committee discretion. The application is considered complete without an interview, and the committee states it has sufficient information in most cases to reach a decision without one. Harvard makes two things clear about its interview logistics: it is not possible for applicants to request an interview, and Harvard does not conduct interviews in Cambridge. If an alumnus reaches out to schedule one, the guidance is to accept the invitation. Harvard uses nearly 10,000 alumni volunteers globally, but even this large network cannot reach all applicants, particularly in international regions.

Key Takeaway:
Not receiving a Harvard interview is not a rejection signal. Harvard cannot interview every applicant and bases the assignment on geography and committee needs. If offered an interview, always accept. Do not attempt to arrange one yourself.

source_name: Harvard College, "Application Tips: Advice to Hold Onto and Remember" (Harvard student blog)
text:
Source:
Harvard College, "Application Tips: Advice to Hold Onto and Remember" (Harvard student blog)
https://college.harvard.edu/student-life/student-stories/application-tips-advice-hold-and-remember

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
A Harvard student offering application tips described the interview as a chance to speak with someone and share who you are as a person. The student's core advice was not to be nervous, framing the interview as just a conversation whose purpose is to help the admissions committee put a face to the application. The student recommended preparing for standard interview questions such as telling the interviewer about yourself, discussing strengths and weaknesses, and explaining why Harvard is the right fit. The most important underlying principle, the student emphasized, is to reflect genuinely on yourself as a person and to give answers that come from real conviction rather than performed impressiveness. Harvard is looking for students with genuine passions and real interests, and the interview is the place to show that those things are authentic.

Key Takeaway:
The Harvard interview is a conversation designed to confirm that the person behind the application is as real and compelling as the materials suggest. Prepare for the standard questions, but prepare by actually reflecting on your genuine interests and values rather than by scripting impressive-sounding answers.

source_name: Veritas Essays blog, "How I Got Into Harvard" (admitted Harvard student's first-person account)
text:
Source:
Veritas Essays blog, "How I Got Into Harvard" (admitted Harvard student's first-person account)
https://veritasessays.org/college-admissions-blog/posts/how-i-got-into-harvard

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
A Harvard student who later accessed their admissions file described learning that their interview and essay were the two factors that distinguished their application and secured their acceptance. The interviewer's written report noted three specific qualities. First, the applicant's structured thinking and organization when answering questions: the student had spent the week before the interview practicing how to walk through their background by telling a coherent story. The interviewer noted this could have come across as rehearsed but was saved by the genuine enthusiasm the student showed. Second, the coherent narrative the applicant built connecting their different high school interests, debate and STEM research, into a single forward-looking set of goals at Harvard, specifically mentioning the Berkman Center for Internet and Policy as a resource directly relevant to their academic plan. Third, the easy flow of conversation: the student had researched the interviewer beforehand and knew she worked in health policy. When she asked about their interest in statistics, the student turned the question back on her and asked how statistics factored into her work. This created a genuine back-and-forth dialogue and kept the interviewer engaged throughout. The overall assessment the interviewer wrote was that this was the most exceptional candidate she had seen in almost a decade of interviewing.

Key Takeaway:
The three qualities that earned the most exceptional rating in this Harvard interview report were structured thinking, a coherent narrative connecting past experiences to specific Harvard resources, and a conversational flow driven by genuine curiosity about the interviewer. Research your interviewer beforehand, turn the interview into a dialogue, and tie your interests to specific Harvard programs.

source_name: CollegeVine blog, "The Harvard Interview: What It's Like, from a Harvard Student" (Lily Calcagnini, Harvard student)
text:
Source:
CollegeVine blog, "The Harvard Interview: What It's Like, from a Harvard Student" (Lily Calcagnini, Harvard student)
https://blog.collegevine.com/the-harvard-interview-what-its-like-from-a-harvard-student

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
A Harvard student described the interview as approximately 45 minutes of pointed questions about the applicant's interests and experiences as they appeared on the Common App, followed by 15 minutes open for the applicant's questions. Harvard interviewers read the applicant's materials in advance, which means they will not ask for a basic description of activities but rather for reflection on them. The student emphasized that there is no correct answer to these questions as long as the response is genuine, but the interview is not the time to consider for the first time what you learned from an experience. That reflection must happen before. Harvard's interview is sometimes conducted by a panel of two alumni rather than one, which the student described as an advantage rather than a disadvantage: it gives the applicant two perspectives on Harvard's community and two opportunities to ask substantive questions. The student's overall message was that interviewers are alumni who once stood in the same shoes as the applicant.

Key Takeaway:
Harvard interviewers have read your application. They are asking you to reflect, not to describe. Do your reflective thinking before the interview, not during it. A two-person panel is not more intimidating than a one-on-one conversation; treat it as access to two Harvard experiences at once.

source_name: AdmitBeacon.com, "Harvard University Interview: Process, Questions and Tips" (March 2026)
text:
Source:
AdmitBeacon.com, "Harvard University Interview: Process, Questions and Tips" (March 2026)
https://www.admitbeacon.com/feeds/blog/harvard-university-interview

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Analysis of the Harvard interview process noted an important structural detail: unlike some other Ivy League schools, Harvard alumni interviewers do not have access to the applicant's full application file before the meeting. They receive only the applicant's name, contact information, and high school name. This means the Harvard interview is less about confirming or elaborating on what is already in the application and more about discovering the person from scratch. The implication for preparation is significant: applicants should be ready to introduce themselves, explain their interests, and build a coherent picture of who they are without being able to assume the interviewer already knows anything about them. Following the Supreme Court's 2023 ruling on affirmative action, Harvard strictly prohibits alumni interviewers from mentioning an applicant's race, ethnicity, or national origin as part of the written report.

Key Takeaway:
Harvard alumni interviewers arrive knowing almost nothing about you. The interview functions as a fresh introduction, not a follow-up conversation about your application. Be prepared to build your portrait from the ground up in 45 to 60 minutes without relying on the interviewer to already know your story.

source_name: IvyCoach, "Harvard Interview Process: Common Questions and Interview Tips" (September 2025)
text:
Source:
IvyCoach, "Harvard Interview Process: Common Questions and Interview Tips" (September 2025)
https://www.ivycoach.com/the-ivy-coach-blog/ivy-league/harvard-interview-process-tips/

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Analysis of the Harvard interview process confirmed that not being offered an interview is not a signal of weakness and has nothing to do with the strength of a candidate. Harvard publishes this directly on its website. In practice, Harvard does not use interviews as a screening tool; they offer an opportunity to share additional context. For students who are offered an interview, the experience is most often with an enthusiastic Harvard alum who is genuinely interested in talking about what they loved about the university. The most effective approach, based on patterns in interview reports, is to come prepared with questions that cannot be answered by searching the Harvard website, to engage the interviewer as a person rather than as an evaluator, and to let your genuine enthusiasm and curiosity do the work.

Key Takeaway:
Harvard alumni interviewers are enthusiastic volunteers, not judges. Come with questions that reflect genuine curiosity about their experience, not questions that could be answered with a Google search. The interview's purpose is additional context, not gatekeeping.

source_name: Yale College Undergraduate Admissions, "Interviews for First-Year Applicants" (official page)
text:
Source:
Yale College Undergraduate Admissions, "Interviews for First-Year Applicants" (official page)
https://admissions.yale.edu/interviews

School:
Yale University

Advice:
Yale's official admissions page on interviews states directly that all Yale interviews are evaluative, distinguishing Yale clearly from schools like Harvard where the weight of the interview is more ambiguous. The admissions committee reads interview reports alongside all other application materials. Yale's Alumni Schools Committee conducts interviews in most parts of the United States and in many foreign countries. A small group of current Yale seniors also serve as senior interviewers and conduct virtual interviews with selected applicants regardless of geographic location. Applicants may not request an interview and should not contact the ASC. Single-Choice Early Action interviews are scheduled before Regular Decision interviews. Not receiving an interview does not disadvantage an applicant, but declining one when offered is inadvisable.

Key Takeaway:
Yale interviews are explicitly evaluative and the report is reviewed as part of the formal file. This distinguishes Yale from schools where the interview carries lighter weight. Declining a Yale interview invitation is visible to the admissions committee and sends a negative signal.

text:
Source:
Yale College Undergraduate Admissions, "Putting Together Your Application" (official guidance)
https://admissions.yale.edu/advice-putting-together-your-application

School:
Yale University

Advice:
Yale's official application guidance notes that interviews are another way to help an application stand out, and that interviewers can learn about candidates not just from what they say but from the interesting, thoughtful questions they bring to the table. Yale frames the interview as a bidirectional opportunity: the applicant learns about Yale from someone with firsthand experience, and the interviewer learns about the applicant beyond what is visible on the page. Yale's guidance emphasizes that the interview is not a standalone evaluation but part of a comprehensive, holistic picture of the applicant.

Key Takeaway:
Thoughtful questions you bring to the interview are explicitly noted by Yale as a way interviewers assess you. The interview is not just about answering questions well; it is also about demonstrating genuine curiosity about Yale and the interviewer's experience.

source_name: CollegeVine blog, "Yale Interview: What It's Like and My Experience" (Michelle Foley, Yale admit)
text:
Source:
CollegeVine blog, "Yale Interview: What It's Like and My Experience" (Michelle Foley, Yale admit)
https://blog.collegevine.com/yale-interview-what-its-like

School:
Yale University

Advice:
A Yale admit described her interview experience in detail. Her interviewer, a Yale alumna named Heather, opened by telling her the interview would be very casual and more like a conversation. The discussion ranged broadly, touching on education systems, favorite teachers, what made their teaching effective, and the student's art practice. When the student mentioned she liked to paint, the interviewer was particularly engaged, noting that artistically inclined students who are also academically invested are unusual at Yale. The student showed her interviewer her art Instagram page, and the interviewer shared a favorite abstract painter of her own. One lesson the student drew from the experience: she got too casual at one point when sharing an amusing anecdote about an English teacher, and the interviewer did not respond well to it. The anecdote was entertaining but not interesting in the way the interview setting requires. The student's core advice was to be yourself, but to know your audience, because the interviewer is older and more experienced, and the interview is the place to show maturity and self-awareness.

Key Takeaway:
Yale interviews really are conversational, but casual does not mean anything goes. The interviewer is still evaluating your maturity, self-awareness, and genuine engagement with ideas. Sharing entertaining anecdotes that are not substantively interesting wastes the time and goodwill that should be directed toward showing who you really are.

source_name: Quora, account from a Yale alumni interviewer responding publicly about what Yale interviewers look for
text:
Source:
Quora, account from a Yale alumni interviewer responding publicly about what Yale interviewers look for
https://www.quora.com/What-do-Yale-admissions-interviewers-look-for

School:
Yale University

Advice:
A Yale alumni interviewer shared their perspective on what distinguishes standout applicants. They described the interview's basic question as whether you would be someone they would want to have a meal with in the dining hall. The interviewer asked whether the applicant seemed genuinely passionate about what they claimed to care about, and whether they came across as a kind person rather than someone likely to be cutthroat in a competitive academic environment. The same source noted that Yale's interview is specifically not a series of scripted questions: when they conducted their own interview as an applicant, the interviewer opened by saying the floor was entirely open and to say whatever they liked. This format rewards genuine self-awareness and the ability to talk about yourself naturally rather than the ability to deliver polished, memorized answers. Students who engaged in a genuine intellectual back-and-forth rather than performing answers impressed interviewers far more than those with technically correct but rehearsed responses.

Key Takeaway:
Yale interviewers are essentially asking whether you would be a good addition to the Yale community at a human level. Genuine passion, intellectual curiosity, and kindness matter as much as any formal credential. The open-ended format of some Yale interviews rewards authentic self-expression over scripted answers.

source_name: Princeton Alumni Weekly, "The Alumni Interview Endures" (in-depth feature article, March/April 2024)
text:
Source:
Princeton Alumni Weekly, "The Alumni Interview Endures" (in-depth feature article, March/April 2024)
https://paw.princeton.edu/article/alumni-interview-endures

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
Princeton's Dean of Admission Karen Richardson described what alumni interview write-ups do for the admissions file. Admissions officers do not have the chance to sit down with applicants themselves, and the alumni write-up brings the story to life in ways the written application cannot. It can confirm or disprove the narrative visible in the essays and recommendation letters, and helps the committee figure out how a particular student might contribute to the dynamic community already at Princeton. The article also profiled Fred Frelinghuysen, an interviewer of seventeen years, who described his approach as trying to get a sense of what the university didn't have in an applicant's written application: the numbers and recommendation letters were already there; what he wanted to understand was what this person was actually like. Robert Bernstein, a Princeton alumnus who was deeply affected by his own interview and later became an interviewer himself, described the most memorable part of his own experience as the authenticity and sincerity of the questions and the genuine interest his interviewer showed in him as a person.

Key Takeaway:
Princeton interviews serve to bring the written application to life and to surface qualities not visible on the page. Alumni interviewers approach the conversation not as judges but as reporters trying to capture who a student is beyond their credentials. The best interviews leave both parties with a sense of genuine human connection.

source_name: Princeton Alumni Weekly, "Interviewers: Ambassadors for Princeton" (letter from a 17-year Princeton interviewer)
text:
Source:
Princeton Alumni Weekly, "Interviewers: Ambassadors for Princeton" (letter from a 17-year Princeton interviewer)
https://paw.princeton.edu/inbox/interviewers-ambassadors-princeton

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
A Princeton alumnus who had served as an alumni interviewer for seventeen years described his goal in each interview as serving as an ambassador who tries to add color to the application that the admissions office may not otherwise have. He described his role as keeping interviews informative, helpful, and positive regardless of the applicant's credentials, so that every student, whether admitted or not, left feeling good about both the conversation and Princeton. He also noted that the intrinsic value of an interview extends beyond the immediate decision: a student who has an excellent interview but is not admitted may later return to Princeton for graduate study, as a postdoc, or as faculty. The relationship between an applicant and Princeton begins at the interview, not the acceptance letter.

Key Takeaway:
Princeton interviewers approach their role as ambassadors, not evaluators. The interview is also a moment to show applicants what Princeton is, not just to discover who the applicant is. Students should approach the conversation as a genuine two-way exchange, not a performance to be judged.

source_name: PrepMaven, "How to Get Into Princeton: 2024-2025" (citing Princeton's official interviewer guide)
text:
Source:
PrepMaven, "How to Get Into Princeton: 2024-2025" (citing Princeton's official interviewer guide)
https://prepmaven.com/blog/applying/how-to-get-into-princeton/

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
Princeton's official guide for alumni interviewers instructs them to think of their role as more akin to an ambassador or a reporter than a judge. The guide also specifies three things interviewers are asked to report on: the candidate's personal qualities with supporting examples when possible; unusual circumstances about the applicant's background, area, or accomplishments that would be helpful for officers to know; and their sense of the student's intellectual curiosity. For intellectual curiosity specifically, the guide asks whether the student can effectively discuss a favorite academic area, describe in some detail a project or paper they found particularly interesting, and identify a book, film, or work of art that has been meaningful and about which they can speak with enthusiasm. A former Princeton alumni interviewer quoted in the analysis confirmed that the goal of any Princeton admissions interview is less about boosting or hurting chances and more about giving applicants a better look at what it is actually like to attend Princeton, while giving the university additional texture about the applicant.

Key Takeaway:
Princeton's interviewers are formally asked to assess three things: personal qualities, unusual contextual information not visible in the file, and intellectual curiosity. Applicants who can discuss a specific academic interest or creative work with genuine enthusiasm are directly responding to what the interviewer's report is designed to capture.

source_name: CollegeVine blog, "Princeton Interview: What It's Like and My Experience" (Priya Desai, Princeton applicant)
text:
Source:
CollegeVine blog, "Princeton Interview: What It's Like and My Experience" (Priya Desai, Princeton applicant)
https://blog.collegevine.com/princeton-interview-what-its-like

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
An applicant who went through the Princeton interview process described the experience as extremely casual and conversational. The interviewer asked about extracurricular involvements, hobbies, and favorite books. The applicant described sharing details about a cultural club and an International Food Fair she had helped organize, which sparked a genuine conversation about the similarities between their respective cultural cuisines. For the favorite book question, she had named a book read for class, which in retrospect she felt was a mistake. Her advice was to discuss a book that is genuinely personally meaningful rather than one that sounds impressive, because the interviewer is looking for authentic response, not performed sophistication. She also reflected that when asked what she was most proud of in high school, she gave an answer that was too general. Her advice was to start with a broader response but then hone in on a specific example with quantifiable impact, following the same principle used in essays.

Key Takeaway:
Specificity and authenticity both matter in the Princeton interview. Generic or overly impressive-sounding answers feel hollow. Name books you actually care about. When discussing accomplishments, move from the general to the specific and include a concrete example of impact.

source_name: Nova Scholar Education, "Mastering Your Princeton Alumni Interview: Tips for Success" (October 2024)
text:
Source:
Nova Scholar Education, "Mastering Your Princeton Alumni Interview: Tips for Success" (October 2024)
https://www.novascholar.org/blog-posts/mastering-your-princeton-alumni-interview-tips-for-success

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
Analysis of the Princeton alumni interview process noted that the alumni interviewer has no access to the applicant's file before the meeting. They have only the applicant's name, email, and high school. This means the Princeton interview, like the Harvard interview, functions as a fresh introduction rather than a follow-up conversation. The analysis recommended preparing by asking what truly excites you about Princeton, being able to speak about your most meaningful experiences, and having two to three questions ready for the interviewer that feel natural based on the conversation and the alumnus's background. Specifically, Princeton-related questions such as what surprised the interviewer most about Princeton after arriving, or what they would tell their freshman self knowing what they know now, tend to generate substantive responses that also demonstrate genuine school-specific knowledge. Attempting to cover more than two or three questions feels like reading from a checklist and should be avoided.

Key Takeaway:
Princeton interviewers come in blind with no file access. Build your introduction from scratch. Prepare two to three organic, substantive questions for the interviewer, not a checklist. Questions about what surprised the alumnus or what they learned at Princeton tend to generate genuine and informative conversations.

source_name: Dartmouth Admissions, "Alumni Interview" (official admissions page)
text:
Source:
Dartmouth Admissions, "Alumni Interview" (official admissions page)
https://admissions.dartmouth.edu/glossary-term/alumni-interview

School:
Dartmouth College

Advice:
Dartmouth's official description of the alumni interview states that it is both informative and evaluative, with the interview report added to the applicant's file and reviewed alongside all other materials. Dartmouth provided sample questions interviewers are expected to ask, including: tell me about your school, courses, teachers, favorite subject, and least favorite subject; describe a project, paper, or lab you were especially proud of completing; and describe your future plans in college and beyond. Students and alumni are prohibited from recording the conversation. If a student prefers not to have an interview, they are asked to respond to the alumnus's outreach as soon as possible to decline, which gives the alumnus time to offer the interview slot to another applicant.

Key Takeaway:
Dartmouth interviews are explicitly evaluative and the report goes directly into the file. Be prepared to discuss specific academic work you are proud of, your school environment, and your future plans in concrete terms. Declining is legitimate but should be done quickly and politely to allow the alumnus to offer the time elsewhere.

source_name: Dartmouth Admissions student blog, "Interview Breakdown: A Guide for Prospective Students" (Paulina Cuadra, Dartmouth student)
text:
Source:
Dartmouth Admissions student blog, "Interview Breakdown: A Guide for Prospective Students" (Paulina Cuadra, Dartmouth student)
https://admissions.dartmouth.edu/follow/blog/paulina-cuadra/interview-breakdown-guide-prospective-students

School:
Dartmouth College

Advice:
A Dartmouth student who had gone through the interview process described the experience from the student's side. The interview is conducted by a current Dartmouth senior or alumnus who is usually from a region near the applicant and is trained to ask questions and assess fit for the college. The tone is informal: admissions officers at Dartmouth want to learn more about who you are as a person and ask students to use the opportunity to share about themselves authentically. The student noted that Dartmouth puts significant effort into organizing and conducting interviews, overseeing an extensive volunteer network, specifically because they want students to have an opportunity to share about themselves in a more informal manner than the written application allows.

Key Takeaway:
Dartmouth's interview is designed to capture who you are as a person in a way the written application cannot. The informal tone is intentional. Students should use the relaxed setting to share genuine parts of themselves rather than defaulting to polished, formal presentation.

text:
Source:
Dartmouth Admissions, "Admissions Ambassador Program: Conducting the Interview" (official interviewer guidance)
https://dartmouth.imodules.com/s/1353/directory/index.aspx?sid=1353&gid=350&pgid=11126

School:
Dartmouth College

Advice:
Dartmouth's official guidance for alumni interviewers instructs them to set a conversational tone, noting that if the interviewer is comfortable and relaxed, the student will likely be as well. The guidance also states that no matter the strength of the applicant, they should leave the interview feeling good about both the conversation and about Dartmouth. This framing makes clear that Dartmouth's interview is also an ambassadorial moment: even applicants who will not be admitted should walk away with a positive impression of the school. The interview is designed to last 30 to 45 minutes, including time for the applicant to ask their own questions. The interviewer guide also provides suggested questions and a sample report form to ensure consistency across the volunteer network.

Key Takeaway:
Dartmouth explicitly trains interviewers to make every applicant feel good about the experience, regardless of admissibility. This means the tone of the interview is not a signal of where you stand. Bring your genuine self, ask your genuine questions, and let the conversation flow naturally.

source_name: Top Tier Admissions, "Alumni Interviews: What to Expect" (February 2025)
text:
Source:
Top Tier Admissions, "Alumni Interviews: What to Expect" (February 2025)
https://toptieradmissions.com/alumni-interviews-what-to-expect/

School:
Yale University and Dartmouth College (both explicitly evaluative)

Advice:
Analysis of Ivy League interview programs noted an important distinction among schools: Yale and Dartmouth are explicitly evaluative interviews where the written report is reviewed alongside the application file. At these schools, unlike schools with non-evaluative or informational interviews, the interview can meaningfully affect how an application is read. The analysis recommended against simply winging the interview even at more informal schools, but was especially emphatic about Yale and Dartmouth: interview preparation should be treated seriously and not dismissed as a formality. The conversational format should not be confused with low stakes. The analysis also recommended being prepared to speak for 30 seconds to a minute in response to each question, practicing this with a friend or mentor in advance, and going into the conversation ready for the alumni to engage more conversationally than a formal interview.

Key Takeaway:
Yale and Dartmouth interviews are explicitly evaluative with written reports reviewed by the committee. Do not mistake the informal, conversational format for low stakes. Prepare seriously, practice speaking in substantive responses, and remember that the casual tone does not mean the interview does not matter.

source_name: Princeton Alumni Weekly, "The Alumni Interview Endures," quoting Karen Richardson, Princeton Dean of Admission (March/April 2024)
text:
Source:
Princeton Alumni Weekly, "The Alumni Interview Endures," quoting Karen Richardson, Princeton Dean of Admission (March/April 2024)
https://paw.princeton.edu/article/alumni-interview-endures

School:
Princeton University, with broader Ivy League context

Advice:
Karen Richardson, Princeton's Dean of Admission, described what the university is trying to understand through the alumni interview. They are trying to figure out, as they build the community, who this student might be and how they might contribute to the dynamic community already there. She also discussed what happens to the interview program in a broader sense: some other selective universities have scaled back or eliminated alumni interviews in recent years, citing the challenges of recruiting enough alumni, the sensitivity of relying on volunteers after the Supreme Court ruling on affirmative action, and logistical complexity at scale. Princeton has chosen to maintain the program because the write-ups can provide vital insights about applicants, while also functioning as an ambassadorial moment for the institution regardless of outcome.

Key Takeaway:
Princeton's stated goal in the interview is to understand how you would contribute to its community, not merely to verify your credentials. The interview helps admissions build a picture of a future Princeton student, not just a qualified applicant pool. Come prepared to articulate not just who you are but what you would bring to and do at Princeton specifically.

source_name: Princeton Alumni Weekly, profile of Cyrus Hatam, Princeton Class of 2023, and his interviewer Robert Bernstein
text:
Source:
Princeton Alumni Weekly, profile of Cyrus Hatam, Princeton Class of 2023, and his interviewer Robert Bernstein
https://paw.princeton.edu/article/alumni-interview-endures

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
Cyrus Hatam, a Princeton Class of 2023 graduate, sent a note to his alumni interviewer Robert Bernstein after graduating. In the note, he wrote that he always told people how their conversation in a Washington coffee shop had gone nearly two hours and taken several tangents, including a discussion about basketball and Arnold Schwarzenegger. The conversation had made such an impression on him that he planned to become an alumni interviewer himself. Bernstein, reflecting on his own interview as a high school senior in Tucson, described being touched that someone showed so much interest in him and lent so much time, with authenticity and sincerity in the questions. He left the interview thinking that regardless of the outcome, the conversation had been wonderful. This account illustrates the dual purpose of the interview: it builds a relationship between the applicant and the institution that persists regardless of the admissions outcome.

Key Takeaway:
The alumni interview can be a genuinely meaningful conversation that leaves a lasting impression on both sides. Approaching it as a human exchange rather than an evaluation, and allowing the conversation to develop naturally including tangents around shared interests, often produces the most memorable and impactful interactions.

source_name: Selective Admissions, "How to Prepare for a College Interview (2025-2026): Expert Tips" (January 2026)
text:
Source:
Selective Admissions, "How to Prepare for a College Interview (2025-2026): Expert Tips" (January 2026)
https://www.selectiveadmissions.com/blog-posts/how-to-prepare-for-a-college-interview

School:
Harvard University, Yale University, Princeton University (and others)

Advice:
Analysis of current interview practices across Ivy League and selective schools found that Columbia phased out its alumni interview program as of the 2023-2024 cycle and is not using interviews for the 2025-2026 cycle. Brown shifted to an optional two-minute video introduction rather than a formal interview. For the schools that continue to use interviews, including Harvard, Yale, Princeton, and Dartmouth, the interview is described as more important than ever as admissions offices look for additional context to support holistic review. For many students, the interview is the most nerve-wracking part of the application process specifically because writing allows time to reflect and revise while the interview requires speaking about yourself in real time, often with someone you have never met.

Key Takeaway:
The interview landscape has diverged significantly across the Ivies. Columbia and Brown have moved away from traditional evaluative interviews. Harvard, Yale, Princeton, and Dartmouth continue to use interviews as meaningful components. Know each school's current interview policy before the application cycle begins, and invest preparation time accordingly.

source_name: The Daily Pennsylvanian, "Alumni Interview Conversation Ends: The Human in College Admissions" (September 2025)
text:
Source:
The Daily Pennsylvanian, "Alumni Interview Conversation Ends: The Human in College Admissions" (September 2025)
https://www.thedp.com/article/2025/09/alumni-interview-conversation-ends-human-college-admissions

School:
University of Pennsylvania (and broader Ivy League context)

Advice:
A reflection piece published in the University of Pennsylvania's student newspaper after Penn ended its alumni conversation program described the broader trend across Ivy League schools. The author noted that as of the 2025-2026 cycle, only Yale and Dartmouth among the Ivies continued to describe their interviews as formally evaluative with written reports alongside the file. Harvard and Princeton's interview processes were described as murky, with availability depending on alumni or the need for additional context. Columbia discontinued alumni interviews in 2023 citing equity and access. Cornell now limits formal video interviewing to its architecture program. Brown replaced alumni interviews with an optional student video introduction. Penn's departure from alumni conversations was described as the end of an era. The piece raised questions about what is lost when the informal human interaction of an interview disappears from a process increasingly dominated by standardized metrics.

Key Takeaway:
The Ivy League interview landscape has fragmented significantly. Only Yale and Dartmouth maintain explicitly evaluative interviews with written reports. Harvard and Princeton's interview processes exist but are not universal. Columbia, Brown, UPenn, and Cornell have significantly reduced or eliminated their interview programs. Students should verify the current interview policy at each target school before applying.

source_name: AdmissionSight.com, "Yale Interview Guide: Sample Questions and Tips" (December 2024)
text:
Source:
AdmissionSight.com, "Yale Interview Guide: Sample Questions and Tips" (December 2024)
https://admissionsight.com/does-yale-interview-everyone/

School:
Yale University

Advice:
Analysis of the Yale interview process offered practical guidance on the most common question Yale applicants face: what does receiving or not receiving an interview mean? The analysis confirmed that Yale prioritizes interviews for students for whom the admissions committee needs more information. Not receiving an interview does not hurt an applicant's chances; many successful applicants are never interviewed. If offered an interview, Yale strongly encourages accepting. The analysis also emphasized that the interview is a genuine opportunity to share leadership, creativity, and problem-solving experiences that might not be fully visible in the written application, and that Yale interviewers are specifically looking for evidence of genuine intellectual curiosity and authentic engagement with the things the applicant claims to care about.

Key Takeaway:
Being offered a Yale interview means the admissions committee wants more information about you, which is a signal of serious consideration but not a guarantee of admission. Not being offered one is not a negative signal. If offered, always accept. Come prepared to demonstrate genuine passion for the activities and academic subjects you have described in your application.

source_name: Leland, "Princeton Interview Guide: Questions, Expert Tips and How to Prepare" (June 2025)
text:
Source:
Leland, "Princeton Interview Guide: Questions, Expert Tips and How to Prepare" (June 2025)
https://www.joinleland.com/library/a/navigating-the-princeton-university-interview-questions-tips-and-insights

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
Expert guidance on preparing for the Princeton alumni interview identified the most common strategic mistakes applicants make. The first is speaking in generalities about why they want to attend Princeton, using statements like wanting a strong education in a beautiful setting or being impressed by the alumni network, which could apply to dozens of schools. Princeton is looking for students who have thought deeply about why Princeton specifically, and who can connect that specificity to their intellectual passions and personal values. The guidance recommended referencing unique Princeton programs such as the junior paper and senior thesis requirement, the Bridge Year program, the Lewis Center for the Arts, or specific residential college traditions rather than generic prestige markers. The second mistake is rattling off a dozen prepared questions like a checklist at the end. The guidance recommended picking two to three questions that arise naturally from the conversation and from the alumnus's background, asking with genuine curiosity, and following up on their answers rather than jumping immediately to the next question.

Key Takeaway:
Generic answers to why Princeton are among the most common interview mistakes. Princeton-specific knowledge of programs, traditions, and culture signals that your interest is genuine and researched, not brand-driven. Two or three thoughtful, natural questions are always better than a prepared list of ten.

Sources include official admissions guidance from Harvard, Yale, Princeton, and other Ivy League schools; first-person accounts from admitted students; data analysis from profiles of admitted students; and publicly available research from the Harvard Graduate School of Education. All advice is paraphrased or summarized from publicly available content. No advice has been fabricated.
source_name:
Harvard College Student Blog, "A Senior's Advice on Applying to College"
Braeden, Class of 2021 Alumni, published December 2020
text:
Source:
Harvard College Student Blog, "A Senior's Advice on Applying to College"
Braeden, Class of 2021 Alumni, published December 2020
https://college.harvard.edu/life-at-harvard/student-stories/seniors-advice-applying-college

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Braeden, a Harvard senior from the first generation program who had spent over three years advising other first-generation students, described his own high school leadership profile as rooted entirely in genuine passion rather than credential-seeking. He cared deeply about his school clubs and had a strong interest in mentorship, but had not accumulated a long list of awards or recognition. What he described as a core insight from watching thousands of students go through the admissions process is that admissions officers can detect when a student lacks genuine passion for their activities, and that authentic commitment to things you actually care about reads far more powerfully than a long list of impressive-sounding but hollow activities. He was explicit: being a national award winner is not a prerequisite for Harvard admission. Demonstrating that you genuinely care about what you do, and that your leadership comes from real investment in the people and causes you serve, is more valuable than any title.

Key Takeaway:
Harvard does not require national-level leadership credentials. What distinguishes admitted students is that their commitment to their activities, including their leadership within them, flows from genuine care rather than credential-building. Passion and authentic engagement are detectable and valued above titles and trophies.

source_name: Princeton University Official Admissions, "Helpful Tips for Applicants"
text:
Source:
Princeton University Official Admissions, "Helpful Tips for Applicants"
https://admission.princeton.edu/apply/before-you-apply/helpful-tips

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
Princeton's official guidance on the application states directly that the admissions committee looks for students who make a difference in their schools and communities. It invites applicants to tell the committee about their leadership activities, interests, special skills, and other extracurricular involvements, and specifically asks students to mention if they have held a job or a responsibility at home. This framing matters: Princeton explicitly counts domestic responsibilities and work experience as legitimate leadership and community contribution. The guidance also emphasizes that what Princeton wants to understand is not just what activities a student participated in, but what commitments they made and what they did to act on those commitments. Leadership at Princeton is framed as a quality that emerges from commitment and action, not merely from holding a formal position.

Key Takeaway:
Princeton defines leadership as the willingness to make commitments and act on them. A student who held a job, cared for siblings, or organized something at their place of worship is demonstrating leadership in Princeton's framework. The committee wants to understand what you actually did, not what title you held.

source_name: Yale University Admissions Office, "Advice on Putting Together Your Application"
text:
Source:
Yale University Admissions Office, "Advice on Putting Together Your Application"
https://admissions.yale.edu/advice-putting-together-your-application

School:
Yale University

Advice:
Yale's official application guidance addresses leadership directly as part of the activities section. Yale states that the committee would like to see that applicants have spent time pursuing meaningful opportunities and that they have had a positive impact on the people around them. Yale also says it expects students to take leadership positions when they are available and to invest their energies into the activities they choose. Two additional phrases in Yale's guidance carry significant weight: that students demonstrate a deep commitment to and genuine appreciation for what they spend their time doing, and that the joy you take in the pursuits that really matter to you will strengthen your candidacy. This framing makes clear that leadership at Yale is not purely about positions or outcomes but about the depth of genuine investment in what you do.

Key Takeaway:
Yale's leadership framework centers on positive impact on others, genuine appreciation for what you do, and demonstrated investment over time. Taking leadership positions matters, but the joy and depth of engagement behind those positions matter just as much, if not more.

source_name: Yale University Admissions Office, "What Yale Looks For"
text:
Source:
Yale University Admissions Office, "What Yale Looks For"
https://admissions.yale.edu/what-yale-looks-for

School:
Yale University

Advice:
Yale's official statement on what it looks for in applicants includes counselor recommendations as part of the picture. Yale describes the role of the counselor recommendation as providing the admissions committee with important context: what a particular leadership position means at a student's specific school, information about the student's background, and textured insight that helps the application come to life. This means that leadership is not evaluated in isolation at Yale. A leadership role at a highly resourced school with dozens of clubs is read differently than the same title at a small school where that student had to build the organization from scratch. The committee's understanding of leadership is explicitly contextual.

Key Takeaway:
Yale evaluates leadership in context. The same title at a well-resourced school versus an under-resourced one can mean very different things. Students at schools with limited opportunities should not assume their leadership carries less weight; in many cases, leading something small in a resource-scarce environment demonstrates more initiative than leading something large in a resource-rich one.

source_name: Karina Macosko, interview published by AcademicInfluence.com, "What Helped Me Get Into Princeton" (updated July 28, 2023)
text:
Source:
Karina Macosko, interview published by AcademicInfluence.com, "What Helped Me Get Into Princeton" (updated July 28, 2023)
https://academicinfluence.com/interviews/college-prep/what-helped-me-get-into-princeton

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
Karina Macosko, who was admitted to Princeton early decision with a 4.7 GPA and as the first in her class, was president of her debate club throughout high school and competed at the national level in track and field. She also worked for AcademicInfluence.com while still in high school, conducting academic interviews as a student contributor. Her leadership profile reflected real commitment to activities she genuinely cared about: she had been involved in debate since early high school, worked at her church, and competed seriously in track. She was explicit in her advice about the biggest myth she heard as a younger student: that you should join clubs or activities because they look good on a college application. She warned that admissions officers can tell the difference between real investment and strategic participation. Her own leadership had emerged from long-term commitment to specific activities she loved.

Key Takeaway:
Leadership that grows organically from multi-year commitment to something you genuinely love is far more compelling than leadership titles strategically added to a resume. Karina's debate presidency and track leadership were both outgrowths of years of authentic involvement, not credentials she assembled for an application.

source_name: Harvard Graduate School of Education, Making Caring Common Project, "Turning the Tide: Inspiring Concern for Others and the Common Good through College Admissions" (January 2016, endorsed by more than 50 universities including Harvard, Yale, and MIT)
text:
Source:
Harvard Graduate School of Education, Making Caring Common Project, "Turning the Tide: Inspiring Concern for Others and the Common Good through College Admissions" (January 2016, endorsed by more than 50 universities including Harvard, Yale, and MIT)
https://mcc.gse.harvard.edu/reports/turning-the-tide-college-admissions

School:
Harvard University, Yale University, and more than 50 endorsing institutions

Advice:
The Turning the Tide report, co-authored by Harvard Graduate School of Education researchers and endorsed by admissions deans from more than 50 universities including all Ivy League schools, called for a fundamental reframing of how community leadership and service are evaluated in college admissions. The report specifically called on students to engage in meaningful, sustained community service that is authentically chosen, consistent, and well-structured and that provides opportunity for reflection. It distinguished this from superficial service activity done purely to list on an application. The report also asked that students take collective action that tackles community challenges, and that they engage in service that is about doing with rather than doing for, meaning collaboration with communities rather than top-down service. The document warned against reducing leadership to an achievement or a credential, arguing that service should be an experience that develops gratitude and a sense of responsibility for the future.

Key Takeaway:
The Harvard-led Turning the Tide initiative, endorsed across the Ivy League, called explicitly for shifting how leadership and community service are evaluated: from quantitative metrics toward authentic, sustained engagement. Collective action that addresses real community challenges is valued more highly than impressive-sounding but hollow service activities.

source_name: Harvard Graduate School of Education, Making Caring Common, extended guidance
text:
Source:
Harvard Graduate School of Education, Making Caring Common, extended guidance
https://www.gse.harvard.edu/ideas/usable-knowledge/16/06/college-and-real-you

School:
Harvard University (and broader Ivy League context)

Advice:
A follow-up piece from Harvard's Making Caring Common project, authored by Richard Weissbourd, who co-directed the initiative and led the Turning the Tide report, addressed the question of what authentic leadership and service mean. Weissbourd was explicit: the goal is to redefine service so it is not considered an achievement but an experience. He noted that community service had historically been framed in terms of leadership and entrepreneurship, turning what should be a genuine orientation toward others into a performance. The report warned students against rushing to join community service organizations or establish a charity in response to admissions pressure. The most meaningful leadership comes from genuine care for a cause or community, sustained over time, and driven by something other than the college application.

Key Takeaway:
Harvard researchers who directly influenced Ivy League admissions criteria explicitly warned against treating leadership and community service as achievements to be accumulated. Leadership that reads as genuine is driven by actual care for others and sustained over time, not assembled in the junior year of high school to strengthen an application.

source_name: AdmitStudio, "How to Get Into Yale: What Actually Works" (December 2025)
text:
Source:
AdmitStudio, "How to Get Into Yale: What Actually Works" (December 2025)
https://admitstudio.com/undergraduate-articles/how-to-get-into-yale/

School:
Yale University

Advice:
Analysis of Yale's admissions approach to leadership, drawing on Yale's own published guidance, identified a specific set of questions Yale uses when evaluating an activity description: did you start a new program? Did you increase participation? Did you secure funding? Did you train new members? Did you change school policy? Did you identify a problem and implement a solution? These questions reflect Yale's definition of meaningful leadership: not holding a title, but initiating change and contributing meaningfully within a context. The analysis noted that describing hours spent is less useful than describing what changed because of your involvement. Yale is explicitly looking for evidence of initiative: students who did not just participate passively but who identified a need and acted on it.

Key Takeaway:
Yale's leadership evaluation centers on whether something changed because of you. Starting a program, increasing participation, securing funding, changing policy, or solving an identified problem are all concrete leadership outcomes Yale is looking for. Hours logged and titles held are secondary to demonstrable change initiated.

source_name: Fortuna Admissions, "Best Extracurricular Activities for Ivy League Schools" (February 5, 2026)
text:
Source:
Fortuna Admissions, "Best Extracurricular Activities for Ivy League Schools" (February 5, 2026)
https://fortunaadmissions.com/college/best-extracurricular-activities-for-ivy-league-schools/

School:
All Ivy League schools (team includes former Ivy League admissions officers)

Advice:
Analysis from Fortuna Admissions, whose team includes former Ivy League admissions officers, identified a specific framework for evaluating leadership that applies across all Ivy League schools. True leadership is not about holding a title; it is about what you do with that position. Admissions officers want to see that a student identified a need, rallied people around a vision, and created measurable impact. Three specific examples of strong leadership stories were identified: a student government president who launches a schoolwide tutoring program that improves GPA averages across multiple grades; a robotics captain who rebuilds a struggling team, introduces new training systems, and leads them to win a regional championship; and a club founder who organizes a community fundraiser raising fifteen thousand dollars to provide technology for underserved schools. The critical advice for framing leadership in an application was to use a clear arc: the problem you saw, the actions you took, and the measurable results you achieved.

Key Takeaway:
Leadership must be framed as an arc in the application, not as a title held. The three-part structure of problem identified, action taken, and measurable results achieved is exactly what Ivy League admissions officers are looking for when they read leadership descriptions. Reflecting on what you learned from the experience adds further depth.

source_name: PrepMaven, "How to Get Into Princeton: 2024-2025" (citing Princeton's official interviewer guide)
text:
Source:
PrepMaven, "How to Get Into Princeton: 2024-2025" (citing Princeton's official interviewer guide)
https://prepmaven.com/blog/applying/how-to-get-into-princeton/

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
Princeton's official guide for alumni interviewers, referenced in PrepMaven's analysis, specifies three things interviewers are formally asked to assess and report on: the candidate's personal qualities with supporting examples, unusual circumstances about the applicant's background or accomplishments that might not be visible in the file, and the student's intellectual curiosity. Notably, intellectual curiosity is defined as the ability to discuss a favorite academic area in depth, describe a project or paper they found genuinely interesting, or identify a book, film, or work of art that has been meaningful to them. This framing reveals that Princeton's conception of leadership-adjacent qualities includes intellectual initiative, not just organizational leadership. A student who has taken genuine intellectual leadership, pursuing learning deeply and independently, is demonstrating exactly the kind of engagement Princeton values.

Key Takeaway:
Princeton evaluates leadership across multiple dimensions including intellectual leadership, not just organizational positions. A student who has read widely, conducted independent inquiry, or developed a distinctive intellectual perspective is demonstrating leadership in Princeton's framework, even without a club presidency.

source_name: CollegeVine Blog, "Why Colleges Want to See More Than Class President on Your Applications"
text:
Source:
CollegeVine Blog, "Why Colleges Want to See More Than Class President on Your Applications"
https://blog.collegevine.com/why-colleges-want-to-see-more-than-class-president-on-your-applications

School:
All Ivy League schools (general analysis)

Advice:
Analysis of how competitive colleges evaluate leadership found that traditional leadership titles like class president, football captain, and similar roles are not ineffective but are significantly limited as differentiators because they are common among competitive applicants at every elite college. These roles, when they appear on applications, do not demonstrate a particular academic or personal niche, which is an important differentiator at highly competitive institutions. The analysis recommended that students look outside of school for leadership opportunities if none exist at school, or consider starting something new at school that fills a visible gap. Starting a new club requires dedication, demonstrates initiative, and shows that a student is invested in their school and willing to step up when they see something missing. This form of entrepreneurial leadership carries more differentiating weight than holding a pre-existing position.

Key Takeaway:
Class president and team captain are not weak leadership credentials, but they are common. What distinguishes applicants is leadership that fills a gap, creates something new, or connects to a distinctive personal or academic niche. Starting something, even small, demonstrates initiative in a way that simply occupying a pre-existing role does not.

text:
Source:
Quora, response from admitted student about the importance of leadership experience in college admissions
https://www.quora.com/How-important-is-leadership-experience-in-college-admissions

School:
All Ivy League schools (general analysis from former applicants and insiders)

Advice:
An analysis shared publicly on Quora by someone familiar with Ivy League admissions identified several legitimate forms of leadership beyond traditional club titles. These included family responsibilities, part-time work with supervisory responsibilities, and organizing support within immigrant or underserved communities, all of which constitute leadership when framed around responsibility, influence, and outcomes. The analysis noted that context matters critically: leading a major city-wide nonprofit as a high school junior weighs differently than leading a small club at a resource-rich school, but both are evaluated by scale, challenge, and initiative relative to what was available. Leadership that is informal, intellectual, or rooted in community contribution is fully legitimate, provided it is articulated in terms of what changed because of the applicant's involvement.

Key Takeaway:
Family responsibilities, work with supervisory elements, and community organizing all constitute leadership in the Ivy League admissions framework, provided they are articulated in terms of initiative, impact, and outcomes. Context is evaluated: leadership at a school with limited resources signals more about character than the same title at a school where that leadership came easily.

CollegeAdvisor.com, Harvard, Yale, and Princeton College Panel webinar (August 17, 2023)
Featuring Theodore Longlois (Harvard), Stacey Tuttle (Yale), and Manuel Gomez Castano (Princeton)
text:
Source:
CollegeAdvisor.com, Harvard, Yale, and Princeton College Panel webinar (August 17, 2023)
Featuring Theodore Longlois (Harvard), Stacey Tuttle (Yale), and Manuel Gomez Castano (Princeton)
https://www.collegeadvisor.com/webinars/harvard-yale-and-princeton-college-panel/

School:
Harvard University, Yale University, Princeton University

Advice:
In a panel webinar featuring admitted students from Harvard, Yale, and Princeton, Theodore (Harvard, Class of 2016) described his own extracurricular approach as rooted in activities he genuinely cared about, noting that there was no formula and that you can do pretty much anything as long as you are passionate about it and can show leadership, time management, and contribution. He specifically warned against joining a club just to get into the Ivy League, stating that admissions officers detect the difference between genuine involvement and strategic padding. Another panelist who had done varsity sports discussed how athletics helped build the leadership qualities of teamwork, sacrifice, and discipline that formed part of a cohesive applicant profile. The key insight from all three panelists was that leadership was read not as an isolated credential but as evidence of character traits that would make the student a valuable member of their college community.

Key Takeaway:
Admitted Harvard, Yale, and Princeton students all identified the same pattern: leadership was most effective when it grew from genuine care and was legible as an expression of character, not as a strategic credential. Activities that demonstrate leadership, time management, and contribution to others can come from any domain, provided the investment is real.

text:
Source:
IvyWise, "Why Leadership Matters in College Admissions" (February 4, 2025)
https://www.ivywise.com/blog/why-leadership-matters-in-college-admissions/

School:
All Ivy League schools (analysis by former admissions officers)

Advice:
Analysis by IvyWise, whose counseling staff includes former Ivy League admissions officers, described the core principle of leadership in college admissions: it is about more than holding a title. It is about how you make an impact on your classmates, school, and community. The analysis identified multiple forms of leadership that colleges value highly, including mentoring younger or less experienced members of a team or club, which builds communication skills while fostering community; organizing events like fundraisers or competitions, which teaches project management and problem-solving; working with different groups to coordinate efforts, which enhances collaboration; and setting specific, measurable goals for a group and working toward them, which demonstrates strategic planning and perseverance. A key point was that demonstrated leadership often stems from a genuine passion for a particular area, and that this genuine foundation is what makes the leadership legible as character rather than strategy.

Key Takeaway:
Mentoring, organizing, coordinating, and goal-setting within a group are all legitimate leadership expressions that colleges value highly, even without a formal title. Leadership with genuine passion behind it reads as a character trait, not just an activity, and that distinction matters enormously to admissions committees.
