Harvard Graduate School of Education, Making Caring Common, Turning the Tide (context on equity in research access)
Cross-referenced with: MIT Dean of Admissions Stuart Schmill, quoted in ProPublica investigation (2023)
text:
Source:
Harvard Graduate School of Education, Making Caring Common, Turning the Tide (context on equity in research access)
Cross-referenced with: MIT Dean of Admissions Stuart Schmill, quoted in ProPublica investigation (2023)
https://mcc.gse.harvard.edu/reports/turning-the-tide-college-admissions

School:
Harvard University, and broader Ivy League context

Advice:
MIT's Dean of Admissions and Student Financial Services Stuart Schmill acknowledged in a 2023 ProPublica investigative report on high school research publication that research is one of the activities Ivy League admissions offices are very aware is not offered equitably. Access to university laboratories, faculty mentorship, and structured research programs is heavily correlated with family resources and geographic proximity to major research institutions. Students from rural areas, low-income backgrounds, or under-resourced schools are less likely to have formal research opportunities available through their school or community. Ivy League schools, Schmill suggested, are aware of this inequity and evaluate research in context. A student who conducted independent research in their backyard or community, driven by genuine curiosity with no institutional support, is demonstrating something as valuable as a student who had access to a university lab, possibly more.

Key Takeaway:
Admissions offices at top institutions explicitly acknowledge that research access is inequitable. Students who lack formal research opportunities are not disadvantaged if they demonstrate equivalent intellectual initiative through self-directed projects, independent inquiry, or community-driven investigations. Context matters as much as the formal credential.

source_name: Pioneer Academics, "Is That High School Research Paper Authentic? 7 Things to Consider" (November 27, 2025)
text:
Source:
Pioneer Academics, "Is That High School Research Paper Authentic? 7 Things to Consider" (November 27, 2025)
https://pioneeracademics.com/news/is-that-high-school-research-paper-authentic/

School:
All Ivy League schools (analysis aimed at admissions officers)

Advice:
A detailed analysis published by Pioneer Academics, which has over a decade of experience in ethical high school research programs, described a growing tension in how research is evaluated in Ivy League admissions. A 2023 joint investigation by ProPublica and The Chronicle of Higher Education exposed a fast-growing industry that sells publication as an outcome, commodifying what should be a meaningful intellectual journey. The analysis found that the best research projects are not primarily about publication; they show real curiosity and thought, connect to what the student actually cares about, reflect genuine effort and initiative, and demonstrate that the student understands how research actually works. Admissions offices are now aware of the difference between authentic research and purchased publication, and admissions officers are specifically looking for evidence that the research reflects the student's genuine interests rather than a strategic credential.

Key Takeaway:
Ivy League admissions offices have become increasingly aware of the difference between authentic research and credential-purchased publication. Students who publish in low-quality journals or describe research that does not connect authentically to their stated interests risk raising red flags rather than adding credibility. Authentic research, even unpublished, is far more valuable than a published paper that lacks genuine intellectual origin.

source_name: Pioneer Academics, extended analysis of authenticity in high school research (citing ProPublica/Chronicle investigation)
text:
Source:
Pioneer Academics, extended analysis of authenticity in high school research (citing ProPublica/Chronicle investigation)
https://pioneeracademics.com/helpful-resources/publishing-as-high-school-students/the-truth-about-high-school-research-publication-what-you-must-know/

School:
All Ivy League schools

Advice:
Analysis of research publication practices in high school admissions found that publication by a high school student has become so common that it is no longer inherently differentiating. What matters is where the student publishes, what the research demonstrates about their thinking, and whether the research authentically connects to who they are as an applicant. Reputable journals that publish high school student research include The Concord Review for history essays, the Journal of Emerging Investigators for biological and physical sciences, and the Regeneron Science Talent Search for STEM research. The analysis warned specifically against predatory journals, which capitalize on students' ambitions by publishing any paper for a fee without genuine peer review. Admissions officers have become sophisticated readers of these credentials and can detect the difference between a paper in a legitimate peer-reviewed journal and a paper in a pay-to-publish outlet.

Key Takeaway:
Publication venue matters significantly in Ivy League admissions. Reputable journals like The Concord Review, the Journal of Emerging Investigators, and Regeneron STS carry genuine weight. Papers in pay-to-publish or low-credibility journals can actively harm an application by signaling credential-seeking over authentic inquiry. When in doubt, an unpublished paper completed under a real mentor with genuine intellectual stakes is more valuable.

text:
Source:
The Concord Review, as described in HelloCollege's guide to high school research publication (October 2025)
https://sayhellocollege.com/blog/how-to-publish-research-as-a-high-schooler/

School:
All Ivy League schools (especially applicable to humanities-focused applicants)

Advice:
The Concord Review is described in multiple sources as the most prestigious journal in the country for high school students, accepting only about five percent of submissions. Its prestige comes from its rigorous selectivity, the high quality of writing expected (average papers run around nine thousand words), and its long history of contributors going on to top universities. The journal publishes historical essays only, which makes it particularly relevant for students who are humanities-focused but want a research credential. Its selectivity means that even unsuccessful submissions demonstrate serious intellectual engagement with historical methodology; the process of writing a substantial, properly sourced historical essay at the standards the journal requires is itself evidence of the kind of scholarly capability Ivy League schools most value in humanities applicants.

Key Takeaway:
For humanities-focused applicants, The Concord Review represents the gold standard of high school research publication. Even the process of attempting a submission at that level, writing a thoroughly researched nine-thousand-word historical essay, demonstrates scholarly ambition that will read clearly in an application regardless of publication outcome.

source_name: Polygence data analysis, survey of 590 seniors from the 2022-23 application cycle
text:
Source:
Polygence data analysis, survey of 590 seniors from the 2022-23 application cycle
https://www.polygence.org/blog/research-projects-high-school-college-admissions

School:
All Ivy League schools (data-driven analysis)

Advice:
In a survey of five hundred ninety high school seniors from the 2022-2023 college application cycle, students who featured research guided by a Polygence mentor on their applications reported Ivy League admission rates approximately five times higher than the national average, at twenty-four and four tenths percent versus five percent nationally. Polygence also found that for over eighty percent of their students, the research project they completed influenced what they ultimately chose to study in college. These findings suggest that research that is genuinely connected to a student's intellectual interests, persistent enough to shape their academic trajectory, and substantial enough to anchor a portion of their application has a measurable effect on admissions outcomes. The analysis also noted that a former undergraduate admissions officer at Cornell described students with tangible indicators of their passions, projects, experiments, and portfolios, as far more memorable than equally qualified students who lacked them.

Key Takeaway:
Data from the 2022-23 cycle suggests that research connected to genuine interests and substantial enough to influence future academic direction is associated with significantly higher Ivy League admission rates. Research should not be a short-term credential activity but a sustained inquiry that genuinely shapes how you think about your field.

https://cambridge-research.org/blogs/guide-to-showcasing-your-ccir-research-on-us-college-applications/

School:
University of Pennsylvania (and broader Ivy League context)

Advice:
According to data cited by the Cambridge Centre for International Research, approximately thirty percent of UPenn's admitted students showcased research projects in their applications. This figure is consistent with broader trends showing that research experience, while not required, is prevalent enough among admitted Ivy League students that applicants without any form of independent inquiry are at a meaningful disadvantage at the most competitive schools. The same analysis noted that admissions officers may not have time to read a lengthy paper in detail, making it essential to highlight the publication, mentor reference, and key findings clearly in the application. The essay and activity descriptions should explain what the research was about, why it mattered to the student, and what the student learned from the process, not just state that a paper exists.

Key Takeaway:
Approximately one third of UPenn admitted students presented research experience in their applications, suggesting that independent inquiry is a mainstream rather than exotic credential at the Ivy League level. Presentation matters: the essay and activity description accompanying research should explain its meaning and the student's intellectual growth, not just its existence.

source_name: IvyMax, "How Important is High School Research in 2026?" (March 2026)
text:
Source:
IvyMax, "How Important is High School Research in 2026?" (March 2026)
https://ivymax.com/blog/cpp/importance-of-high-school-research/

School:
All Ivy League schools

Advice:
Analysis of the current admissions landscape found that for students targeting top-twenty universities and Ivy League institutions, independent research has transitioned from a nice-to-have bonus to what the analysis described as an unspoken prerequisite. The analysis cited UPenn data showing that nearly one third of all admitted students engaged in formal academic research during high school, and noted that for highly competitive STEM and pre-med applicants at Ivy League schools, the percentage of admitted students with formal research experience is likely significantly higher. However, the analysis also identified a common ceiling students hit: most start with AP coursework, enter a local science fair, and attempt a basic paper, but then reach a point where they lack the network to access university laboratories or the mentorship to push their work toward more formal academic venues. Breaking through that ceiling requires either formal mentorship, self-directed inquiry that does not depend on lab access, or field-work and data collection in the community.

Key Takeaway:
For competitive STEM and pre-med applicants to Ivy League schools, research experience is becoming an effectively required credential. Students who recognize this early in high school have time to develop genuinely meaningful research; students who start late must focus on independent, community-based inquiry that does not require institutional access.

source_name: Solomon Admissions Consulting, "Cornell University Admissions Guide" (citing Cornell's internal rubric for intellectual vitality)
text:
Source:
Solomon Admissions Consulting, "Cornell University Admissions Guide" (citing Cornell's internal rubric for intellectual vitality)
https://www.solomonadmissions.com/undergrad/schools/cornell-university/

School:
Cornell University

Advice:
Analysis of Cornell's internal admissions evaluation criteria identified a rating scale that values demonstrated love of learning. The highest level of academic engagement is described as an intense love of learning, as evidenced by academic research outside the classroom with a college professor leading to publication in a journal. Intermediate levels describe students who do independent reading outside the classroom but lack published research with a faculty mentor. The analysis noted that Cornell has a two-part evaluation system assessing applicants independently on academics and on potential contributions to campus, with intellectual vitality appearing in both. Students who frame their research experience in terms of how it connects to Cornell's specific undergraduate research programs, including the Engineering Learning Initiatives or the Office of Undergraduate Research, make the most effective use of this credential in the application.

Key Takeaway:
Cornell's internal evaluation criteria explicitly distinguish between students who read independently and students who have produced original research with faculty mentorship. Publication with a real faculty mentor in a legitimate venue is described at the highest level of academic engagement Cornell can recognize. Framing your research in relation to Cornell's specific research programs strengthens the application further.

text:
Source:
Immerse Education, "High School Research Journals for Future Scholars" (January 28, 2026)
https://www.immerse.education/knowledge-base/high-school-research-journals-future-scholars/

School:
All Ivy League schools

Advice:
Analysis of how published research is evaluated in college admissions found that a published paper in a reputable venue demonstrates to Ivy League admissions officers several distinct qualities: that the applicant can manage long-term projects, analyze data, and communicate complex ideas across an extended time horizon. These are skills that go beyond what classroom performance typically demonstrates. A paper also distinguishes the applicant from others with similar grades and test scores by providing independent evidence of intellectual engagement. The analysis also noted that universities increasingly value research experience in holistic admissions reviews, and that platforms like the Journal of Student Research and The High School Journal carry weight because they hold students to scholarly expectations. The key is that the journal must have genuine peer review standards; a paper in a self-published or pay-to-publish venue does not carry the same meaning.

Key Takeaway:
A published paper in a journal with genuine peer review standards demonstrates project management, analytical capability, and intellectual communication across an extended time horizon. These are qualities that academic transcripts cannot fully capture. The value comes from the journal's standards, not from the fact of publication itself.

Scholar Launch, "How to Publish High School Research: Your Comprehensive Guide" (December 4, 2025)
https://www.scholarlaunch.org/blog/how-to-publish-high-school-research-your-comprehensive-guide

School:
All Ivy League schools

Advice:
A comprehensive guide to high school research publication described the process and context of getting published as an applicant to top universities. Publishing academic articles can increase an applicant's chances by showcasing initiative, maturity, and an advanced skill set that admissions officers are looking for. The guide emphasized that the goal is not publication for its own sake but the development of genuine scholarly skills: the ability to formulate a question, review existing literature, design a methodology, analyze results, and communicate findings clearly. Students who go through a real peer review process, receiving critical feedback and revising their work, are demonstrating exactly the scholarly resilience and intellectual maturity that Ivy League schools value. The process itself, even when it results in rejection, is a meaningful experience that shapes how the student writes about their research interests in their essays.

Key Takeaway:
The peer review process, including revision based on critical feedback, is a valuable experience in its own right. Students who have experienced real scholarly review and revision can write about their research with a specificity and intellectual maturity that students who only participated in structured programs cannot. The process is as important as the outcome.

source_name: Regeneron International Science and Engineering Fair (ISEF), as analyzed in PrepScholar and Ivy Scholars guides (2024)
text:
Source:
Regeneron International Science and Engineering Fair (ISEF), as analyzed in PrepScholar and Ivy Scholars guides (2024)
https://blog.prepscholar.com/international-science-and-engineering-fair

School:
All Ivy League schools (particularly STEM-focused applicants)

Advice:
The Regeneron International Science and Engineering Fair is described as the most prestigious pre-university scientific and engineering research competition in the world, with approximately seventeen hundred finalists competing annually from more than sixty countries. Earning a spot in the top one hundred competitors at ISEF is considered enough to make a college application stand out even to Ivy League admissions offices. The competition requires students to conduct original research under the mentorship of an expert, then present their findings to judges who are themselves field experts. The process of qualifying for ISEF, which involves first winning local and regional science fairs, demonstrates sustained commitment to a research project across multiple years. The Regeneron Science Talent Search, the companion competition specifically for high school seniors, is similarly regarded as one of the most prestigious academic competitions in the United States for STEM applicants.

Key Takeaway:
For STEM-focused applicants to Ivy League schools, ISEF or Regeneron STS recognition is among the most powerful research credentials available. Even qualifying at the regional level demonstrates original research conducted under expert mentorship and the ability to present findings to professional audiences. These competitions validate that the research was genuinely original and competitively evaluated.

text:
Source:
Essai.in, "9 Under-the-Radar Journals Where Students Can Publish Humanities Work" (November 2025)
Cross-referenced with Immerse Education and other sources on connecting research to Ivy League applications
https://essai.in/student-journals-humanities-research/

School:
All Ivy League schools (especially humanities and social-science applicants)

Advice:
A guide to humanities research publication for prospective Ivy League students identified several practical strategies for connecting research to applications. Once a student has published or substantially completed a research project, they should include it in their LinkedIn, personal website, or application portfolio alongside a reflection on what the project taught them. The research should also be referenced in supplemental essays, connecting it specifically to the academic programs and faculty at the schools they are applying to. Recommenders should be asked to comment on the research experience in their letters, as independent corroboration of the research's quality from a teacher or mentor adds significant credibility. Students who continue contributing to academic or scholarly communities after their initial research, by joining a journal as an editor or contributor in college, for instance, demonstrate ongoing intellectual engagement that reinforces the signal the research sends.

Key Takeaway:
Research is most effective in an Ivy League application when it is consistently referenced across multiple components: the essay, the supplemental, the recommender letters, and the activity list. A single mention of a publication is less powerful than a coherent narrative in which the research appears as a central expression of your intellectual identity throughout the application.

source_name: Harvard College, Official FAQ: "How Will the Admissions Committee Evaluate My Test Scores?"
text:
Source:
Harvard College, Official FAQ: "How Will the Admissions Committee Evaluate My Test Scores?"
https://college.harvard.edu/resources/faq/how-will-admissions-committee-evaluate-my-test-scores

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Harvard's official FAQ on test score evaluation describes how the admissions committee reads scores in the context of each application as a whole. Harvard states directly that it has found SAT and ACT scores to be the best predictors of Harvard grades. At the same time, the committee explicitly acknowledges that not all students attend well-resourced schools, and that students from modest economic backgrounds or first-generation college families may have had fewer opportunities to prepare for standardized tests. Every application is read with attention to the context of the student's high school and community, with recognition that talent is everywhere but opportunity and access are not. The score is a data point, not the whole picture, but it is a meaningful and consistent one that helps the committee compare students across vastly different academic environments.

Key Takeaway:
Harvard considers test scores one of its most reliable predictors of academic success, but reads scores in the context of a student's school and community. A score that looks modest in the abstract may look genuinely strong when placed in its proper educational context. Access and opportunity matter in how scores are evaluated.

source_name: Harvard College, Official FAQ: "Why Did Harvard Decide to Require Standardized Testing Again?"
text:
Source:
Harvard College, Official FAQ: "Why Did Harvard Decide to Require Standardized Testing Again?"
https://college.harvard.edu/resources/faq/why-did-harvard-decide-require-standardized-testing-again

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Harvard's official explanation for reinstating the testing requirement, published in April 2024, stated that the decision was motivated by research confirming standardized testing's predictive value for both college and post-college success. The explanation also noted that when students had the option to withhold their scores, some chose not to submit information that, when interpreted in the context of the local norms of their school, could have helped their application. Harvard was explicit: more information, especially highly predictive information, is valuable for identifying talent across the socioeconomic spectrum. The FAQ further stated that this was particularly true for low-income and minority applicants who may also be disadvantaged in other aspects of their application due to lack of academic opportunity.

Key Takeaway:
Harvard reinstated testing not just to screen applicants but specifically because withholding scores was harming some lower-income and minority students whose scores, when evaluated in context, would have strengthened their cases. Students from under-resourced schools with respectable scores should submit them, even if those scores fall below Harvard's median.

source_name: Harvard Gazette, "Harvard Announces Return to Required Testing" (April 2024)
text:
Source:
Harvard Gazette, "Harvard Announces Return to Required Testing" (April 2024)
https://news.harvard.edu/gazette/story/2024/04/harvard-announces-return-to-required-testing/

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
When announcing the return to required testing, Harvard's Dean of the Faculty of Arts and Sciences Hopi Hoekstra explained the reasoning directly. Standardized tests provide information that is predictive of success in college and beyond for all students regardless of their background and life experience. She noted that the research cited included a study by Harvard economists Raj Chetty, David Deming, and colleague John Friedman, using data from more than 400 institutions and about 3.5 million undergraduates annually. That research showed that standardized test scores, unlike high school grades alone, were highly predictive of post-college success. Chetty's own comment on the findings was notable: he acknowledged that students from higher-income families often have greater access to test prep resources, but noted that other admissions measures such as recommendation letters, extracurriculars, and essays were even more prone to such wealth-correlated biases. Requiring test scores was therefore likely to make the admissions process more meritocratic, not less.

Key Takeaway:
The faculty research cited by Harvard found that test scores are better predictors of college and post-college success than other application components, and that essays, recommendations, and extracurriculars show greater correlations with family income than test scores do. This research fundamentally reframed how Harvard thought about the equity implications of test-optional policies.

source_name: Harvard Crimson, "The Return to Test Requirements Shrank Harvard's Applicant Pool" (November 2025)
text:
Source:
Harvard Crimson, "The Return to Test Requirements Shrank Harvard's Applicant Pool" (November 2025)
https://www.thecrimson.com/article/2025/11/4/test-requirements-harvard-admissions-effect/

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
The Harvard Crimson reported that nearly three-quarters of students admitted to Harvard's Class of 2028 submitted SAT or ACT scores even during the test-optional period, suggesting that the most competitive applicants were already choosing to submit. When Harvard reinstated testing for the Class of 2029, application volume dropped. The Crimson noted ongoing academic debate about the value of test scores: a 2025 Berkeley study found that high school GPA was a better predictor of first-year college performance than standardized testing, while the Harvard Kennedy School research cited in Harvard's announcement concluded the opposite when looking at longer-term outcomes. The practical implication for applicants is that scores matter meaningfully at Harvard but are evaluated within a holistic framework that considers every other aspect of the application alongside them.

Key Takeaway:
Even during Harvard's test-optional years, most competitive applicants submitted scores. The return to required testing reflects institutional confidence in scores as a predictive tool. Students whose scores are strong should submit; the scores contribute to a fuller picture of academic preparation.

Oriel Admissions, "SAT Score Ranges for the Ivy League: Middle 50% Bands and Strategy" (May 2026)
Citing Common Data Set 2023-2024 reports for all eight Ivy League schools
https://orieladmissions.com/sat-score-ranges-ivy-league/

School:
All Ivy League schools

Advice:
Analysis of Common Data Set data for all eight Ivy League schools revealed that admitted student SAT ranges cluster tightly across the cohort. The middle-50 percent SAT bands, based on 2023-2024 CDS data, were approximately as follows: Harvard, Yale, and Princeton each showed a 25th percentile of roughly 1490 to 1500 and a 75th percentile of 1580. Columbia and UPenn showed a range of approximately 1490 to 1570. Brown and Dartmouth showed ranges of approximately 1500 to 1560. Cornell, due to its multi-college structure, showed the widest variation with a 25th percentile near 1470 and a 75th percentile near 1550. For unhooked applicants, meaning those without legacy status, recruited athlete status, or other institutional hooks, the analysis recommended targeting 1560 or above for Harvard, Yale, and Princeton, and 1530 to 1550 for the remaining Ivies. Critically, the analysis also noted that published mid-50 percent ranges climbed roughly 50 points at the 25th percentile since 2018-2019, driven by self-selection during the test-optional years when mostly high scorers chose to submit.

Key Takeaway:
Ivy League SAT ranges are compressed at the top of the national distribution. The mid-50 percent bands across all eight schools span roughly 1470 to 1580. A 1480 SAT that once placed students comfortably inside the middle 50 percent at Harvard and Princeton before the pandemic now falls below the 25th percentile at both schools. Target ranges have shifted significantly upward.

source_name: NextAdmit.com, "Harvard SAT Scores: Full Breakdown" (March 2025)
text:
Source:
NextAdmit.com, "Harvard SAT Scores: Full Breakdown" (March 2025)
https://nextadmit.com/blog/harvard-sat-scores/

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Analysis of Harvard's published score data found that the average SAT score among admitted students hovers around 1550, with the 25th percentile at approximately 1500 and the 75th percentile at approximately 1570. Harvard does not publish a minimum score and explicitly states it does not admit by the numbers. For ACT, the middle 50 percent of admitted students falls in the range of 34 to 36. Harvard superscores the SAT, meaning it takes the highest section scores from all test dates, which makes strategic retaking a viable approach. There is no evidence of a preference for one test over the other; Harvard accepts both equally. Applicants are encouraged to choose whichever test they score higher on relative to that test's percentile distribution.

Key Takeaway:
Harvard's median admitted SAT is approximately 1550 and median ACT is approximately 35. Harvard superscores the SAT across test dates. There is no preference for SAT over ACT. Students should choose whichever test format produces their strongest results, and retaking to improve specific sections is a legitimate and common strategy.

text:
Source:
AdmissionSight.com, "Is Harvard Test Optional?" (September 2025)
https://admissionsight.com/is-harvard-test-optional/

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Analysis of Harvard's Common Data Set for 2023-2024 found that most admitted Harvard students score between 34 and 36 on the ACT, with the 25th percentile at 34 and the 75th percentile at a perfect 36. On the SAT, over 92 percent of admitted students score between 700 and 800 on the evidence-based reading and writing section, and 96 percent score in that range for math. The analysis recommended that students use official SAT and ACT practice tests from the College Board and Khan Academy as the most reliable prep resources, avoiding lower-quality third-party test prep books. If a student's SAT and ACT scores are not strong, the recommendation was to refocus preparation energy on the essays, extracurriculars, and recommendations that can still set an application apart, rather than exhausting time and energy on repeated test attempts with diminishing returns.

Key Takeaway:
Harvard's admitted score ranges are exceptionally high, with the vast majority of admits scoring in the 700 to 800 range on each SAT section. Official practice resources from the College Board and Khan Academy are the most efficient prep tools. Students who have plateaued below the competitive range should redirect effort to other application components.

source_name: Dartmouth College Student Newspaper, "College to Reinstate Standardized Test Requirement for Class of 2029" (The Dartmouth, February 2024)
text:
Source:
Dartmouth College Student Newspaper, "College to Reinstate Standardized Test Requirement for Class of 2029" (The Dartmouth, February 2024)
https://www.thedartmouth.com/article/2024/02/college-to-reinstate-standardized-test-requirement-for-class-of-2029

School:
Dartmouth College

Advice:
Dartmouth's reinstatement of the testing requirement, the first among the Ivy League in 2024, was based on a faculty study by economics professors Elizabeth Cascio, Bruce Sacerdote, and Douglas Staiger, along with sociology professor Michele Tine. Their research concluded that standardized test scores are an important predictor of student success in Dartmouth's curriculum regardless of a student's background or family income. Dartmouth's Dean of Admissions Lee Coffin noted in the announcement that for many lower-income applicants, submitting a test score, even one below Dartmouth's median, would have confirmed their academic qualifications and could have tripled their chances of admission. Coffin specifically said Dartmouth was missing great students by not having that information, particularly students from high schools where admissions officers might not have clear context for evaluating grades alone.

Key Takeaway:
Dartmouth's reinstatement was driven by evidence that lower-income students with competitive scores were withholding them, inadvertently hurting their own applications. Students from less-resourced schools should not assume their scores are too low to submit. A score evaluated in the context of a student's school and background may be far more impressive than the raw number suggests.

source_name: Dartmouth faculty research findings, cited in The Dartmouth and City Journal (February 2024)
text:
Source:
Dartmouth faculty research findings, cited in The Dartmouth and City Journal (February 2024)
https://www.thedartmouth.com/article/2024/02/sharma-students-share-varied-reactions-to-reinstatement-of-sat-and-act-admissions-requirement

School:
Dartmouth College

Advice:
The Dartmouth faculty research found that SAT scores explained approximately 22 percent of the variation in students' first-year GPAs at Dartmouth, while high school GPA explained only 9 percent. The researchers also found that students with a perfect 4.0 in high school earned college grades that were only 0.1 points higher than students with a 3.2 high school GPA, suggesting that grade inflation had severely diminished the usefulness of GPA alone as a predictor of college performance. During the test-optional years, 46 percent of Dartmouth applicants declined to submit their scores. Among those who withheld scores, many had scores of 1450 or above, which would have been competitive at Dartmouth in the context of their background. The research concluded that SAT scores were a better predictor than high school grades, essays, or teacher recommendations of how well students would perform in Dartmouth's rigorous curriculum.

Key Takeaway:
Dartmouth's internal data showed that test scores predicted college academic performance more reliably than any other single admissions component, including GPA. Grade inflation has made GPA a weak differentiator. Students who withhold competitive scores because they assume them insufficient are making a systematic error that damages their own applications.

source_name: NPR, transcript of coverage of Dartmouth's announcement (February 2024)
text:
Source:
NPR, transcript of coverage of Dartmouth's announcement (February 2024)
https://www.npr.org/transcripts/1229223433

School:
Dartmouth College

Advice:
Dartmouth economics professor Bruce Sacerdote, who led the faculty research, described to NPR what the data revealed. Admissions officers would see in the data that a student from a disadvantaged background had scored 1450 or 1500, but because the student had not submitted their score, the office had no way of knowing this. Such a student was not admitted to Dartmouth despite having a score that, in the context of their background, represented an outstanding academic achievement. Sacerdote described this as the university missing out on great students because of a policy designed to help them that ended up working against them. He emphasized that at Dartmouth, admissions officers consider scores relative to the local norms of the applicant's high school, which means a score below the median can still be a genuine asset when evaluated fairly.

Key Takeaway:
Contextual evaluation of scores is real and consequential at Dartmouth. Admissions officers do not simply compare scores to an absolute benchmark; they compare them to the local norms of each applicant's school. This means that a 1450 from a low-resourced high school may be as impressive as a 1550 from a heavily resourced prep school.

source_name: Inside Higher Ed, "Yale Adopts Test-Flexible Admissions Policy" (February 2024)
text:
Source:
Inside Higher Ed, "Yale Adopts Test-Flexible Admissions Policy" (February 2024)
https://www.insidehighered.com/news/admissions/traditional-age/2024/02/23/yale-adopts-test-flexible-admissions-policy

School:
Yale University

Advice:
Yale's February 2024 announcement of its test-flexible policy explained the reasoning in detail. During the test-optional years, Yale's experience of reviewing applications without test scores taught the university that requiring only the SAT or ACT before 2020 had likely discouraged some promising students from applying. The test-flexible approach was designed to address this by allowing students to submit SAT, ACT, AP, or IB exam scores, whichever best represented their abilities. Yale cited data suggesting that test scores were the single greatest predictor of a student's future Yale grades. The policy change was described as an attempt to maintain the predictive and comparative value of test scores while reducing the barrier created by requiring only one specific type of test.

Key Takeaway:
Yale's test-flexible policy reflects a genuine effort to improve both the quality of academic evidence in applications and the accessibility of the process. Students who have strong AP or IB scores but weaker SAT or ACT performance now have a legitimate path to demonstrating academic readiness through subject-area exams they may have already taken.

source_name: Yale College Undergraduate Admissions, Letter to School Counselors (February 2024)
text:
Source:
Yale College Undergraduate Admissions, Letter to School Counselors (February 2024)
https://admissions.yale.edu/test-flexible-counselors

School:
Yale University

Advice:
Yale's official letter to school counselors explaining the new test-flexible policy detailed how it would work in practice. Starting with the 2024-2025 admissions cycle, all applicants were asked to choose which scores they wanted considered from four options: SAT, ACT, AP, or IB. Students who chose to submit AP or IB scores were required to include results from all subject exams completed before applying. Yale would allow superscored results from the SAT or ACT, provided all subsections were included. Predicted IB scores would not fulfill the requirement. Yale also addressed students who lacked access to any testing: applicants at schools that did not offer AP or IB courses would not be penalized for being unable to submit those alternatives. Yale's stated goal was to empower applicants to put their best foot forward, recognizing that no single test is a perfect or one-size-fits-all demonstration of college readiness.

Key Takeaway:
Yale's test-flexible policy is not a loophole but a genuine recognition that different students have access to different types of rigorous academic assessment. Students who do well on AP exams in subjects related to their intended area of study now have a legitimate and sometimes stronger way to demonstrate academic preparation than a single standardized test date.

text:
Source:
Yale College Undergraduate Admissions, "Standardized Testing for Transfer Applicants" (official page)
https://admissions.yale.edu/standardized-testing-transfer-applicants

School:
Yale University

Advice:
Yale's official guidance on standardized testing noted that its score ranges provide a snapshot of the class, not a floor for competitiveness. Yale explicitly stated that it is not the case that scores below a certain threshold automatically hurt an application while those above automatically help. Scores below published ranges can still help establish an applicant's academic preparation for Yale coursework, particularly when those scores are evaluated in the context of the student's school and background. This guidance also addressed the practical question of whether to submit: Yale advised students who are uncertain about submitting to note that Yale's admissions committee reads scores in context and that withholding a score that, when contextualized, would strengthen an application is a common mistake.

Key Takeaway:
Yale does not have a hard score cutoff below which an application is automatically disadvantaged. Scores below the published middle-50 percent range can still strengthen an application when evaluated in context. Students from less-resourced schools should not withhold scores reflexively just because those scores fall below the published median.

source_name: Princeton Alumni Weekly (PAW), "Princeton Admissions Will Again Require Standardized Tests in 2027" (October 2025)
text:
Source:
Princeton Alumni Weekly (PAW), "Princeton Admissions Will Again Require Standardized Tests in 2027" (October 2025)
https://paw.princeton.edu/article/princeton-admissions-will-again-require-standardized-tests-2027

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
Princeton's October 2025 announcement that it would require standardized tests starting with the 2027-2028 admissions cycle was based on a review of five years of test-optional admissions data. Princeton found that academic performance at the university was stronger for students who submitted test scores than for students who did not. Approximately 22 percent of the Class of 2029, the most recent class admitted under test-optional policies, did not submit test scores, and that group showed weaker academic performance on average. Princeton's Dean of Admission Karen Richardson had said in 2021 that she did not think the test-optional policy would be permanent, calling testing an important piece of the holistic review process. Princeton waited until after its peers had reinstated testing before making its own announcement, citing the same underlying institutional data as Harvard, Dartmouth, and Yale.

Key Takeaway:
Princeton's internal data confirmed what Dartmouth, Harvard, and Yale had already found: students who submitted test scores performed better academically than those who did not. Students applying to Princeton for the 2026-2027 cycle still have the flexibility of test-optional, but the trend is clear: Princeton expects to have scores for the vast majority of competitive applicants.

source_name: The Princetonian, "Princeton to Require SAT or ACT Scores for Applicants Starting Fall 2027" (October 2025)
text:
Source:
The Princetonian, "Princeton to Require SAT or ACT Scores for Applicants Starting Fall 2027" (October 2025)
https://www.dailyprincetonian.com/article/2025/10/princeton-news-sat-act-standardized-test-optional-required-admissions

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
The Princetonian reported that even during Princeton's test-optional years, 77 to 78 percent of enrolling freshmen chose to submit test scores voluntarily. This self-selection pattern meant that the students not submitting scores were disproportionately those with lower scores, which partially explains the performance gap Princeton found in its data. This dynamic, seen at every Ivy League school that went test-optional, was one of the key drivers of reinstatement across the cohort: when high-scoring students voluntarily submit and lower-scoring students do not, the school still receives test data from most competitive applicants, but the threshold of what gets evaluated as a full application shifts. Students who submitted strong scores during the optional period likely benefited from doing so, as those scores provided an additional positive data point in an already competitive pool.

Key Takeaway:
Even during test-optional years at Princeton, more than three-quarters of enrolled students submitted scores voluntarily. High-scoring students who withheld scores during optional cycles may have missed an opportunity to strengthen their applications. When in doubt and scores are competitive, submitting is generally the right choice.

source_name: Oriel Admissions, "The Return of Standardized Testing: Which Top Colleges Require SAT/ACT" (March 2026)
text:
Source:
Oriel Admissions, "The Return of Standardized Testing: Which Top Colleges Require SAT/ACT" (March 2026)
https://orieladmissions.com/colleges-require-sat-act/

School:
All Ivy League schools (policy overview)

Advice:
A comprehensive analysis of the Ivy League testing policy landscape as of 2026 confirmed that six of the eight Ivy League schools required standardized test scores for first-year applicants for the 2026-2027 cycle. Harvard, Yale (test-flexible), Dartmouth, Brown, Cornell, and Penn all require scores. Princeton is test-optional for one final cycle before requiring scores starting in 2027-2028. Columbia is permanently test-optional. The analysis also noted the consequence for score ranges: because test-optional policies caused high-scoring students to continue submitting while lower-scoring students often withheld, the published middle-50 percent score ranges climbed roughly 50 points at the 25th percentile since 2018-2019. A 1480 SAT that sat comfortably inside the middle 50 percent at Harvard, Yale, and Princeton before the pandemic now falls below the 25th percentile at all three.

Key Takeaway:
The effective floor for competitive SAT scores at the most selective Ivy League schools has risen approximately 50 points since before the pandemic, driven by the self-selection dynamics of test-optional years. Students preparing for 2026 or 2027 cycles should target ranges based on current published data, not pre-pandemic benchmarks.

source_name: Cosmic College Consulting, "SAT Score Needed to Get Into Princeton 2026"
text:
Source:
Cosmic College Consulting, "SAT Score Needed to Get Into Princeton 2026"
https://www.cosmic.nyc/blog/sat-score-needed-to-get-into-princeton-2026

School:
Princeton University

Advice:
Analysis of Princeton's score data for recent admitted classes noted that even during the test-optional period, 77 percent of enrolling freshmen submitted scores. Students scoring 1500 or above were encouraged to submit regardless of the test-optional status. Students below 1450 with other compelling application strengths might weigh the tradeoffs more carefully during optional cycles, but even then, the analysis noted that for most students, the score would still be one positive data point even if not a dominant one. For students applying early action, the self-selected pool tends to have near-perfect scores by the fall of senior year, meaning the competitive bar within the early round is particularly high on test scores.

Key Takeaway:
The practical guidance for Princeton is simple for most students: if your score is 1500 or above, submit it. The early action applicant pool at Princeton is especially competitive on test scores, reflecting a self-selected group of students who have typically achieved strong academic profiles before the fall of senior year.

source_name: PrepMaven, "What's a Good SAT Score for Ivy League Schools? (2026 Data + Expert Advice)"
text:
Source:
PrepMaven, "What's a Good SAT Score for Ivy League Schools? (2026 Data + Expert Advice)"
https://prepmaven.com/blog/test-prep/good-sat-score-for-ivy-league/

School:
All Ivy League schools

Advice:
Analysis of IPEDS data for students enrolling in fall 2023 found that at nearly every Ivy League school, the top 25 percent of admitted students submitted a perfect score of 800 on the SAT math section. For reading and writing, the picture was slightly less uniform but still extremely high: at Dartmouth and Yale, a 740 on reading and writing placed a student only at the 75th percentile of the admitted class. The analysis recommended using the middle-50 percent range as the most practical benchmarking tool, with a clear framework: if your score falls above the 75th percentile for a given school, test scores are an active asset; if your score falls inside the middle 50 percent, scores are neutral and other application components need to differentiate you; if your score falls below the 25th percentile, test scores become a weakness that other strengths need to compensate for.

Key Takeaway:
Use the middle-50 percent framework to categorize your score position at each target school. A score above the 75th percentile is an active asset. A score inside the range is table stakes that does not differentiate. A score below the 25th percentile is a weakness requiring compensation from other application components.

source_name: Tutor Doctor, "SAT Scores for Ivy League: Harvard, Yale, Princeton and More (2025 Data)" (January 2026)
text:
Source:
Tutor Doctor, "SAT Scores for Ivy League: Harvard, Yale, Princeton and More (2025 Data)" (January 2026)
https://www.tutordoctor.com/blog/2026/january/sat-scores-for-ivy-league-harvard-yale-princeton-more-2025-data/

School:
All Ivy League schools

Advice:
Analysis of SAT score ranges across the Ivy League identified three practical tiers for positioning. Scores between 1550 and 1600 place students in excellent shape for any Ivy League school, representing the top tier of the competitive range. Scores between 1500 and 1550 represent the sweet spot for Ivy League applicants, roughly the 50th percentile of admitted students at most Ivies. Students scoring here have cleared the academic bar and can compete effectively when other application elements are strong. Scores between 1450 and 1500 keep students in the conversation but require significant compensatory factors such as exceptional extracurriculars, a distinctive background or perspective, or demonstrated impact that distinguishes the applicant beyond academics. The analysis also reiterated the core lesson from the institutional research at Dartmouth, Harvard, and Yale: test-optional policies hurt the students they were designed to help, because lower-income students systematically underestimated the value of their scores.

Key Takeaway:
The three-tier framework for SAT positioning at Ivy League schools is: 1550 to 1600 is strong at every school; 1500 to 1550 is competitive when the rest of the application is strong; 1450 to 1500 requires significant compensatory factors. Below 1450, students face meaningful academic headwinds at the most selective Ivies.

text:
Source:
ScoreSmart, "What Is a Good SAT Score for Ivy League Schools?" (April 2026)
https://score-smart.com/what-is-a-good-sat-score-for-ivy-league-schools/

School:
All Ivy League schools

Advice:
Analysis of Ivy League score data found that at every Ivy League school, 25 percent or more of enrolled students submitted a perfect 800 on the SAT math section. On the reading section, the 75th percentile at Dartmouth and Yale required a 740. This concentration of top-decile scorers reflects the extreme self-selection of the applicant pool: students applying to Ivy League schools who take the SAT typically score significantly higher than the national average, and admitted students represent the very top of that already-high pool. The analysis emphasized that a strong SAT score does not guarantee admission but does remove a potential weakness and earns an application a complete review. An application below the academic floor at any Ivy League school receives abbreviated consideration regardless of how strong the essays and extracurriculars are.

Key Takeaway:
A competitive SAT score is not a guarantee of full consideration but a prerequisite for it. Scores below the 25th percentile threshold mean an application may not receive a full holistic review regardless of other strengths. Reaching the academic floor through strong scores and course rigor is the entry requirement for everything else to matter.

source_name: Acely, "Getting Into Harvard: Acceptance Rates and Admission Requirements" (November 2025)
text:
Source:
Acely, "Getting Into Harvard: Acceptance Rates and Admission Requirements" (November 2025)
https://acely.com/blog/getting-into-harvard-acceptance-rates-and-admission-requirements

School:
Harvard University

Advice:
Admissions analysis noted that at Harvard, the 25th to 75th percentile SAT range for admitted students is approximately 1460 to 1570. A student aiming to be competitive rather than merely eligible should target the 75th percentile, which is 1570 or above. For ACT, the 25th to 75th percentile range is approximately 33 to 35, with students aiming to be competitive targeting a 35 or perfect 36. The analysis also emphasized that a top GPA and a 1550 SAT do not guarantee admission when everyone applying has near-perfect scores. Harvard's holistic review at that point shifts to essays, extracurriculars, character, and the overall coherence of the application. Being at or above the 75th percentile removes test scores as a weakness and gives the rest of the application the chance to do its work.

Key Takeaway:
At Harvard, a score at the 75th percentile or above removes test scores as a concern and lets other application elements compete on their own terms. Students should aim for at least the 75th percentile at their target schools, not just the median, to ensure their scores function as an asset rather than a neutral factor.

source_name: IvyWise, "What Is a Good SAT Score in 2026?" (March 2026)
text:
Source:
IvyWise, "What Is a Good SAT Score in 2026?" (March 2026)
https://www.ivywise.com/blog/good-sat-score/

School:
All Ivy League schools

Advice:
IvyWise's analysis emphasized the importance of using school-specific middle-50 percent ranges rather than any abstract notion of a good score. A 1400 score, which sounds high, sits at the 95th percentile nationally but falls well below the 25th percentile of admitted students at every Ivy League school. This mismatch between national percentile ranking and school-specific percentile ranking is one of the most common misconceptions students bring into the Ivy League application process. The practical benchmarking framework recommended is simple: find the middle-50 percent range for each target school from the Common Data Set, check which tier your current score falls into, and use that assessment to make realistic decisions about where to apply and how much energy to invest in retesting.

Key Takeaway:
National test score percentiles are misleading in the Ivy League context. A score that is impressive nationally may still be below the 25th percentile of any Ivy League school's admitted class. Always benchmark using each school's published middle-50 percent range, not national averages.

source_name: SparkAdmissions, "What Is a Good SAT Score for Ivy League Admissions?" (August 2025)
text:
Source:
SparkAdmissions, "What Is a Good SAT Score for Ivy League Admissions?" (August 2025)
https://www.sparkadmissions.com/blog/what-is-a-good-sat-score-for-ivy-league/

School:
All Ivy League schools

Advice:
SparkAdmissions offered strategic guidance on retaking the SAT or ACT. Taking the test multiple times to achieve a target score is a legitimate and common strategy, but the analysis recommended limiting retakes to three or four attempts total. Excessive retakes beyond that number may signal a lack of preparation or become a diminishing investment of time that would be better redirected toward essays, extracurriculars, or other components. Superscoring, available at most Ivy League schools for the SAT, means that students can strategically target specific sections in separate test dates rather than needing to improve both sections simultaneously in a single sitting. Even a perfect score of 1600 does not guarantee Ivy League admission; scores are a necessary but not sufficient condition.

Key Takeaway:
Retaking the SAT or ACT is a legitimate strategy, but three to four attempts is a reasonable ceiling. Beyond that, time is better invested in other application components. Superscoring at most Ivies means section-specific improvement across multiple test dates counts, making focused retakes targeting one weak section a more efficient strategy than full retakes.

source_name: Applerouth Tutoring, "Dartmouth Reinstates SAT/ACT Requirements" (February 2024)
text:
Source:
Applerouth Tutoring, "Dartmouth Reinstates SAT/ACT Requirements" (February 2024)
https://www.applerouth.com/blog/dartmouth-reinstates-sat-act-requirements

School:
Dartmouth College

Advice:
Summary of Dartmouth Dean of Admissions Lee Coffin's comments during the reinstatement announcement noted that for many lower-income applicants, submitting a test score even below Dartmouth's median could have confirmed their academic qualifications and dramatically improved their admission odds. Coffin specifically described cases where students from high schools with limited context visible to admissions officers could use a test score as a clear, objective signal of preparation that the transcript alone could not provide as clearly. Elite private schools have guidance counselors who write detailed letters explaining the rigor of their school's curriculum; students from under-resourced public schools often lack that context. A test score fills a critical contextual gap for those students.

Key Takeaway:
For students from schools where admissions officers have limited context about curriculum rigor, a test score provides an objective signal of academic preparation that no other component can substitute for as effectively. Students from under-resourced schools who have scores at or above the school's local-context threshold should almost always submit.

source_name: Oriel Admissions, "The Return of Standardized Testing: Which Top Colleges Require SAT/ACT" (March 2026)
text:
Source:
Oriel Admissions, "The Return of Standardized Testing: Which Top Colleges Require SAT/ACT" (March 2026)
https://orieladmissions.com/colleges-require-sat-act/

School:
All Ivy League schools (Columbia and Princeton policy comparison)

Advice:
As of the 2026-2027 cycle, Columbia remains the sole Ivy League school with a permanent test-optional policy. Columbia's permanently test-optional stance reflects a philosophical difference: Columbia described its application design as affording applicants the greatest possible opportunity and flexibility to represent themselves, stating that students who choose not to submit test scores will not be at a disadvantage. For students applying to Columbia specifically, the analysis noted that while scores will still be reviewed if submitted and may help strengthen an application, Columbia is genuinely committed to evaluating applications on all other components. Princeton, by contrast, ended its test-optional run with the 2026-2027 cycle and requires scores starting in 2027-2028, citing five years of internal data showing stronger academic performance among students who submitted scores.

Key Takeaway:
Columbia is the only Ivy League school where a test-optional strategy is permanently supported by institutional policy. For all other Ivy League schools, submitting a competitive score strengthens an application and, for schools that require testing, is now mandatory. Know each school's specific current policy before making submission decisions.
